metadata.title: AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner (CLF-C02)
metadata.description: Foundational AWS certification covering cloud concepts, security and compliance, AWS core services (compute, storage, networking, databases, AI/ML, analytics, integration), and billing, pricing, and support. Aligned to the official CLF-C02 blueprint with 24% Cloud Concepts, 30% Security and Compliance, 34% Cloud Technology and Services, and 12% Billing, Pricing, and Support.
metadata.subject: Cloud Computing
metadata.author: Dungeon Scholar (sourced from the AWS CLF-C02 exam guide)

knowledgeBase:
=== Domain 1: Cloud Concepts ===

Domain 1: AWS Cloud value proposition. The AWS Cloud trades the capital expense (CapEx) of buying and racking servers for the operational expense (OpEx) of paying only for what you consume, which lets organizations stop guessing capacity, avoid heavy upfront commitments, and redirect undifferentiated heavy lifting like patching power and cooling to AWS. Six advantages are typically cited: trade fixed expense for variable expense, benefit from massive economies of scale, stop guessing capacity, increase speed and agility, stop spending money running and maintaining data centers, and go global in minutes. Elasticity means scaling capacity up and down to match demand, while agility refers to how quickly new resources can be provisioned, often in minutes rather than weeks. High availability uses multiple Availability Zones to keep workloads running through component failures and is a core benefit of building on AWS rather than a single on-premises data center.

Domain 1: AWS global infrastructure. AWS organizes its global footprint into Regions, Availability Zones (AZs), and edge locations, and the relationship between these is one of the most heavily tested concepts on CLF-C02. A Region is a separate geographic area such as us-east-1 (N. Virginia) or eu-west-2 (London) and contains multiple isolated AZs; an AZ is one or more discrete data centers with redundant power, networking, and connectivity, and AZs in a Region are physically separated by meaningful distance but linked by high-bandwidth low-latency networking so that synchronous replication is possible. Edge locations are part of the Amazon CloudFront content delivery network and AWS Global Accelerator, sit closer to end users than Regions, and cache content or terminate connections to reduce latency. Multi-AZ deployments deliver high availability inside a Region; multi-Region deployments are used for disaster recovery, business continuity, low latency for globally distributed users, and meeting data sovereignty requirements that mandate data stay within specific borders.

Domain 1: AWS Well-Architected Framework. The Well-Architected Framework defines six pillars that capture best practices for cloud workloads: Operational Excellence (run and monitor systems to deliver business value), Security (protect data, systems, and assets), Reliability (recover from failures and meet demand), Performance Efficiency (use computing resources efficiently), Cost Optimization (avoid unnecessary spend), and Sustainability (minimize environmental impact of cloud workloads). Each pillar has design principles such as automate with code, scale horizontally to reduce blast radius, stop guessing capacity, and apply security at all layers. The AWS Well-Architected Tool in the console lets architects review workloads against these pillars and produce an improvement plan. Cloud Practitioner candidates do not need to design architectures but must recognize which pillar a given best practice belongs to and the differences between them, for example that encrypting data is Security while right-sizing instances is Cost Optimization.

Domain 1: Cloud economics and TCO. Cloud economics on the exam centers on shifting from fixed CapEx to variable OpEx, removing the cost of unused on-premises capacity, and exploiting economies of scale because AWS aggregates demand across millions of customers. Rightsizing means continuously matching instance type and size to actual workload requirements, and AWS Compute Optimizer makes recommendations for under- or over-provisioned EC2, EBS, Lambda, and Auto Scaling resources. Bring Your Own License (BYOL) lets customers reuse existing software licenses (commonly Microsoft or Oracle) on Dedicated Hosts or Dedicated Instances, while license-included pricing bundles the license cost into the hourly rate of services such as RDS for SQL Server. Total Cost of Ownership comparisons against on-premises must include hardware refresh, real estate, power and cooling, networking, security staffing, and software licensing, not just the headline server cost.

Domain 1: AWS Cloud Adoption Framework and migration strategies. The AWS Cloud Adoption Framework (AWS CAF) organizes cloud transformation across six perspectives: Business, People, Governance, Platform, Security, and Operations, and it identifies expected outcomes such as reduced business risk, improved ESG performance, increased revenue, and improved operational efficiency. Migration strategies are commonly summarized as the seven Rs: Retire (decommission), Retain (keep on-premises), Rehost (lift and shift onto EC2), Relocate (move VMs without conversion via VMware Cloud on AWS), Replatform (lift, tinker, and shift, for example moving SQL Server to RDS), Repurchase (drop and shop, replace with SaaS), and Refactor (re-architect into cloud-native services). AWS Snow Family devices (Snowcone, Snowball Edge) physically transport large datasets to AWS when network transfer is too slow or expensive. AWS DMS and SCT migrate database workloads, including converting schemas between heterogeneous engines such as Oracle to Aurora PostgreSQL.

Domain 1: Elasticity, scalability, agility, and high availability. Scalability is the ability to handle growth by adding resources, and on AWS it is typically achieved horizontally by adding more EC2 instances behind an Elastic Load Balancer rather than vertically by buying a bigger box. Elasticity goes further: it scales capacity up and down automatically with demand using AWS Auto Scaling and target tracking policies so that customers only pay for what they need at any moment. High availability means a workload remains operational despite component failures and is typically met with multi-AZ deployments and load balancers that route around unhealthy targets. Fault tolerance is stronger and means the workload continues with no perceptible degradation even during failure, often requiring active-active multi-Region designs. Agility, often confused with elasticity, refers to how fast teams can experiment and deliver, made possible by self-service provisioning, infrastructure as code with AWS CloudFormation, and managed services that remove undifferentiated work.

=== Domain 2: Security and Compliance ===

Domain 2: AWS shared responsibility model. AWS is responsible for security OF the cloud and customers are responsible for security IN the cloud, and the line between these moves depending on the service abstraction level. AWS owns the physical security of data centers, the hardware, the host operating system, the hypervisor, and the global network, while customers own their data, identity and access management, operating system patches on EC2, network and firewall configuration, client-side encryption, and how applications use AWS services. For managed services like Amazon RDS the customer no longer patches the database engine OS but still controls users, schemas, encryption choices, and IAM policies; for Lambda the customer is responsible only for the function code, IAM permissions, and configuration. Identity and access management, customer data, and proper use of encryption are always the customer's responsibility regardless of service.

Domain 2: AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM). IAM is a global service that manages who (principals) can do what (actions) on which AWS resources, and it is free to use with no charge for users, groups, roles, or policies themselves. Best practices include locking away the root user credentials, enabling multi-factor authentication (MFA) on the root user and all privileged users, granting least privilege through fine-grained policies, using groups to assign permissions to multiple users, and using IAM roles rather than long-lived access keys for workloads such as EC2 instances or Lambda functions. Tasks only the root user can perform include changing the account name and email, closing the account, changing the support plan, and restoring deleted IAM permissions, so the root user should be used only for these specific tasks. IAM policies are JSON documents with Effect, Action, Resource, and optional Condition elements, and they may be AWS managed, customer managed, or inline.

Domain 2: IAM Identity Center and federation. AWS IAM Identity Center (the successor to AWS Single Sign-On) provides centralized workforce identity for accessing multiple AWS accounts and business applications using a single sign-on experience, and it integrates with external identity providers such as Microsoft Active Directory, Okta, and Azure AD via SAML 2.0 or SCIM. Federation lets enterprise users log in with their corporate credentials and assume IAM roles in AWS without creating a separate IAM user for each person, which centralizes lifecycle management and improves auditability. Cross-account IAM roles are the recommended pattern for granting access between AWS accounts so that no long-lived credentials cross the account boundary; the consumer account assumes the role using AWS Security Token Service (STS). Amazon Cognito is a separate service for application end-user identity, including sign-up, sign-in, and federation with social or enterprise providers.

Domain 2: Encryption, KMS, CloudHSM, and Secrets Manager. AWS Key Management Service (KMS) is a managed service for creating and controlling cryptographic keys used to encrypt data at rest across services such as S3, EBS, RDS, and DynamoDB, and it integrates with CloudTrail to record every key use. AWS-owned keys, AWS-managed keys, and customer-managed keys (CMKs) sit on a spectrum of customer control, with customer-managed keys offering the most flexibility for key policies, rotation, and grants. AWS CloudHSM provides single-tenant FIPS 140-2 Level 3 hardware security modules for workloads that require dedicated hardware or specialized cryptographic offload. AWS Secrets Manager stores, rotates, and retrieves secrets such as database credentials and API keys, and it can rotate RDS credentials automatically by invoking a Lambda rotation function; AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store is a cheaper alternative for non-sensitive configuration. Encryption in transit is typically delivered with TLS, and AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) issues and renews public TLS certificates for free for use with Elastic Load Balancers, CloudFront, and API Gateway.

Domain 2: Threat detection, monitoring, and audit services. Amazon GuardDuty continuously analyzes CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs using threat intelligence and machine learning to detect malicious or unauthorized behavior such as cryptocurrency mining or compromised instances. Amazon Inspector automates vulnerability assessment of EC2, ECR container images, and Lambda functions, scoring CVEs and unintended network exposure. Amazon Macie uses machine learning to discover and classify sensitive data such as PII or PHI in S3 buckets. AWS Security Hub is the central dashboard that aggregates findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, IAM Access Analyzer, and partner products, and it checks accounts against standards like CIS AWS Foundations and PCI DSS. Amazon Detective makes it easier to analyze, investigate, and identify the root cause of security findings or suspicious activities by building a graph of behavior. AWS CloudTrail records every AWS API call for governance, compliance, and auditing, while AWS Config records resource configuration changes and evaluates them against rules.

Domain 2: Network protection and DDoS defenses. AWS WAF is a web application firewall that inspects HTTP/HTTPS traffic and lets customers write rules to block SQL injection, cross-site scripting, and other application-layer attacks at CloudFront, Application Load Balancer, API Gateway, or AppSync. AWS Shield Standard is included automatically with all AWS accounts and protects against common network and transport layer DDoS attacks at no extra cost; AWS Shield Advanced is a paid service that adds higher-tier protection, 24x7 access to the Shield Response Team, cost protection against scaling charges during attacks, and tighter integration with WAF and Route 53. AWS Firewall Manager centrally configures and manages WAF rules, Shield Advanced protections, and security group policies across an AWS Organization. Security groups are stateful instance-level firewalls that allow rules only, while network access control lists (NACLs) are stateless subnet-level firewalls that support allow and deny rules and are evaluated in numbered order.

Domain 2: Compliance, governance, and AWS Artifact. AWS publishes its third-party audit reports such as SOC 1, SOC 2, SOC 3, ISO 27001, PCI DSS, FedRAMP, and HIPAA Business Associate Addendum through AWS Artifact, which is the central go-to portal for compliance evidence in the console. AWS Audit Manager continuously collects evidence from AWS usage and maps it to control frameworks like HIPAA, GDPR, and PCI to streamline audit preparation. AWS Config supports compliance use cases by recording configuration history and running managed or custom rules to detect noncompliant resources such as unencrypted EBS volumes or public S3 buckets. AWS Trusted Advisor is a separate service that gives best-practice checks across cost, performance, security, fault tolerance, and service quotas. AWS Organizations adds preventive guardrails through Service Control Policies (SCPs) that set the maximum permissions any account in an organizational unit can have.

=== Domain 3: Cloud Technology and Services ===

Domain 3: Methods of accessing and operating AWS. AWS resources can be created and managed through the AWS Management Console (web GUI for one-off tasks), the AWS Command Line Interface (CLI) for scripted access from a terminal, AWS SDKs for programmatic access from application code in languages like Python, Java, Go, and JavaScript, and infrastructure as code (IaC) tools like AWS CloudFormation and the AWS Cloud Development Kit (CDK). Use the console for ad hoc exploration and one-time setup, the CLI or SDK for repeatable operational scripts, and CloudFormation when you need version-controlled, repeatable deployments that can be replicated across Regions or accounts. Cloud deployment models are described as cloud (everything in AWS), hybrid (workloads split between AWS and on-premises, often connected by AWS Direct Connect, Site-to-Site VPN, AWS Outposts, or AWS Storage Gateway), and on-premises (no AWS footprint). Choosing between these depends on latency, data residency, regulatory needs, and existing investments.

Domain 3: AWS compute services. Amazon EC2 provides resizable virtual machines billed per second (Linux) or per hour, with families optimized for general purpose (T, M), compute (C), memory (R, X), storage (D, I), and accelerated computing (P, G, Inf, Trn). AWS Lambda runs functions in response to events with no server management, billed per request and per gigabyte-second of execution time, and is the canonical serverless compute service on AWS. AWS Fargate runs containers without managing EC2 hosts and is the serverless option for Amazon ECS and Amazon EKS, while ECS itself is the AWS-native container orchestrator and EKS is managed Kubernetes. AWS Elastic Beanstalk is a platform-as-a-service that takes uploaded code and provisions EC2, ELB, and Auto Scaling for you, while Amazon Lightsail offers simple bundled VMs at predictable monthly prices for small workloads. AWS Outposts extends AWS infrastructure into a customer's own data center for low-latency or data-residency needs, and AWS Batch orchestrates large numbers of batch computing jobs.

Domain 3: Auto Scaling and load balancing. AWS Auto Scaling automatically adjusts the number of EC2 instances in a group based on metrics such as average CPU utilization, request count, or custom CloudWatch metrics, and it is one of the primary delivery mechanisms for elasticity on AWS. Elastic Load Balancing (ELB) distributes incoming traffic across multiple healthy targets across Availability Zones and comes in four flavors: Application Load Balancer (ALB) for HTTP/HTTPS at layer 7 with content-based routing, Network Load Balancer (NLB) for TCP/UDP at layer 4 with extreme performance and static IPs, Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB) for inserting third-party network appliances, and the legacy Classic Load Balancer. Combining Auto Scaling and ELB across multiple AZs is the standard pattern for both high availability and elasticity. Health checks let the load balancer stop sending traffic to unhealthy instances and let Auto Scaling replace them.

Domain 3: AWS database services. Amazon RDS is a managed relational database service that supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server, and Db2, and it offers Multi-AZ deployments for high availability and read replicas for read scaling. Amazon Aurora is AWS's MySQL- and PostgreSQL-compatible engine that delivers up to five times the throughput of standard MySQL with a self-healing fault-tolerant storage layer that replicates six copies across three AZs. Amazon DynamoDB is a fully managed serverless NoSQL key-value and document database with single-digit millisecond latency at any scale and built-in encryption, backup, and global table replication. Amazon ElastiCache offers managed Redis OSS and Memcached for in-memory caching to reduce latency and database load. Amazon Neptune is a graph database, Amazon DocumentDB is MongoDB-compatible, and Amazon Redshift is the petabyte-scale data warehouse. AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) moves data between sources and targets with minimal downtime, and AWS Schema Conversion Tool (SCT) converts schemas between heterogeneous engines such as Oracle to PostgreSQL.

Domain 3: AWS network services and the VPC. An Amazon Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) is a logically isolated virtual network defined by a CIDR block, divided into subnets that live in a single Availability Zone, and reachable from the internet through an internet gateway and from on-premises through a virtual private gateway, AWS Site-to-Site VPN, or AWS Direct Connect. Public subnets have a route to an internet gateway; private subnets reach the internet outbound through a NAT gateway. Security groups are stateful and attached to elastic network interfaces, while network ACLs are stateless and attached to subnets. Amazon Route 53 is a highly available authoritative DNS service that supports routing policies including simple, weighted, latency-based, geolocation, geoproximity, failover, and multi-value answer, and it can also register domains. AWS Direct Connect provides a dedicated private network connection between an on-premises facility and AWS, while AWS VPN delivers encrypted tunnels over the public internet.

Domain 3: Content delivery and edge networking. Amazon CloudFront is the AWS content delivery network that caches content at hundreds of edge locations globally to reduce latency and offload origins like S3 buckets, ALBs, and custom HTTP servers, and it integrates with AWS WAF, AWS Shield, and ACM for TLS. AWS Global Accelerator improves availability and performance for global TCP and UDP applications by routing traffic over the AWS global network from edge locations to the nearest healthy endpoint, providing two static anycast IP addresses. AWS Transit Gateway is a regional hub that connects VPCs and on-premises networks at scale, replacing complex VPC peering meshes. AWS PrivateLink exposes services across accounts and VPCs through interface VPC endpoints without traversing the public internet. AWS Client VPN provides remote-access VPN for end users, while Site-to-Site VPN connects whole networks.

Domain 3: AWS storage services and S3 storage classes. Amazon S3 is object storage with eleven nines of durability designed across multiple AZs, organized into buckets and accessed by HTTP APIs. S3 storage classes trade access pattern for cost: S3 Standard for frequently accessed data, S3 Intelligent-Tiering to automatically move objects between tiers based on access patterns, S3 Standard-Infrequent Access (Standard-IA) and S3 One Zone-IA for less-frequent access, S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval for archive with millisecond access, S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval (formerly Glacier) for archive with minutes-to-hours retrieval, and S3 Glacier Deep Archive for the cheapest storage with 12-hour retrieval. S3 Lifecycle policies move objects between classes or expire them automatically. Amazon EBS provides block storage volumes attached to EC2 instances and is tied to a single AZ; instance store is ephemeral block storage on the host. Amazon EFS is a fully managed NFS file system that scales elastically and is shared across many EC2 instances and AZs. Amazon FSx offers managed Windows File Server, Lustre, NetApp ONTAP, and OpenZFS file systems. AWS Storage Gateway provides hybrid file, volume, and tape gateways with cached access to on-premises workloads. AWS Backup centralizes backup policy across many AWS services.

Domain 3: AWS AI/ML and analytics services. Amazon SageMaker AI is the end-to-end platform for building, training, and deploying machine learning models, while higher-level AI services package pretrained models behind simple APIs: Amazon Rekognition for image and video analysis, Amazon Polly for text-to-speech, Amazon Transcribe for speech-to-text, Amazon Translate for language translation, Amazon Comprehend for natural language understanding, Amazon Lex for conversational chatbots that power Alexa, Amazon Textract for OCR and document understanding, Amazon Kendra for enterprise intelligent search, and Amazon Q for generative AI assistants. Amazon Athena queries data directly in Amazon S3 using standard SQL with no servers to manage and pay-per-query pricing. AWS Glue is a serverless data integration and ETL service with a Data Catalog. Amazon EMR runs big data frameworks like Apache Spark, Hive, and Hadoop. Amazon Kinesis ingests and processes streaming data with Data Streams, Data Firehose, and Video Streams. Amazon QuickSight is the serverless BI service for dashboards. Amazon OpenSearch Service runs the OpenSearch search and analytics engine.

Domain 3: Application integration and other in-scope services. Amazon Simple Queue Service (SQS) is a fully managed message queue with Standard (at-least-once, best-effort ordering) and FIFO (exactly-once, strict ordering) options for decoupling producers and consumers. Amazon Simple Notification Service (SNS) is a pub/sub service that fans out a single message to many subscribers including SQS queues, Lambda functions, HTTP endpoints, and email/SMS. Amazon EventBridge is a serverless event bus that routes events from AWS services, SaaS partners, and custom sources to targets using rules and content-based filtering, and is the modern successor to CloudWatch Events. AWS Step Functions orchestrates Lambda and other services into visual state machine workflows. Amazon Connect is a cloud contact center, Amazon Simple Email Service (SES) sends transactional and bulk email, AWS IoT Core connects IoT devices, and Amazon WorkSpaces and AppStream 2.0 deliver virtual desktops and streamed applications to end users. AWS Amplify and AppSync help build frontend and mobile applications with hosted GraphQL and full-stack tooling.

=== Domain 4: Billing, Pricing, and Support ===

Domain 4: AWS pricing fundamentals and free services. AWS pricing follows three principles: pay as you go, pay less when you reserve, and pay even less per unit by using more. There is no charge for creating an AWS account, for IAM, for VPC itself, for AWS Organizations, for Elastic Beanstalk service (only the underlying resources), for CloudFormation templates (only the resources they create), or for AWS Auto Scaling itself. Inbound data transfer to AWS is generally free, while outbound data transfer to the internet is charged per gigabyte and varies by Region; data transfer between AZs in the same Region using private IPs incurs a small per-gigabyte charge, and traffic between Regions also costs money. AWS Pricing Calculator lets you model service costs before deploying anything, and it produces shareable estimates for budgeting and proposals.

Domain 4: EC2 purchasing options. On-Demand Instances charge by the second or hour with no long-term commitment and are best for short, unpredictable, or development workloads. Reserved Instances (RIs) commit to one or three years for a steep discount (up to about 72%) and come in Standard (largest discount, less flexible) and Convertible (smaller discount, can change instance family, OS, tenancy) types; payment can be all upfront, partial upfront, or no upfront. Savings Plans are a more flexible commitment-based discount: Compute Savings Plans (up to 66%) apply to EC2, Fargate, and Lambda regardless of instance family, Region, or OS, while EC2 Instance Savings Plans (up to 72%) lock to an instance family in a Region. Spot Instances offer up to 90% off On-Demand by using spare AWS capacity but can be reclaimed with a 2-minute warning, making them ideal for fault-tolerant batch and big data jobs. Dedicated Hosts give the customer a physical server for BYOL or compliance, while Dedicated Instances run on hardware dedicated to a single customer at the instance level. Capacity Reservations reserve capacity in a specific AZ without requiring a billing commitment.

Domain 4: AWS Organizations, consolidated billing, and cost allocation. AWS Organizations groups many AWS accounts under a single management account and provides consolidated billing, which combines usage from all member accounts to access volume discounts and pooled Reserved Instance and Savings Plans benefits across the organization. Service Control Policies (SCPs) set the maximum permissions for accounts in organizational units (OUs) and are a preventive guardrail rather than a granting mechanism. AWS Control Tower sets up and governs a multi-account environment with prebuilt blueprints based on AWS best practices and a Landing Zone. Cost allocation tags are key/value labels applied to resources, and once activated in the Billing console they let you break down costs in the AWS Cost and Usage Report (CUR) and AWS Cost Explorer by application, project, environment, or owner. AWS-generated tags (such as aws:createdBy) and user-defined tags must both be activated explicitly before they appear in cost reports.

Domain 4: Cost management tools. AWS Cost Explorer provides a console UI for visualizing, understanding, and managing AWS costs and usage over time, with forecasts of future spend and recommendations for Reserved Instances and Savings Plans. AWS Budgets lets you set custom cost or usage budgets and trigger alerts via SNS or email when actual or forecasted spend exceeds thresholds. The AWS Cost and Usage Report (CUR) is the most detailed billing dataset AWS publishes, delivered to S3 and queryable from Athena, QuickSight, or Redshift, and it contains every line item with hourly granularity. AWS Pricing Calculator builds estimates for proposed architectures. AWS Compute Optimizer recommends right-sized EC2, EBS, Lambda, and Auto Scaling configurations based on observed utilization, and AWS Trusted Advisor surfaces idle resources, underutilized instances, and unattached EBS volumes in its cost optimization category.

Domain 4: AWS Support plans. AWS offers five support plans: Basic (free, included with every account, account and billing support only, access to AWS Trusted Advisor core checks), Developer (business hours email access to Cloud Support Associates, suitable for testing and development), Business (24x7 phone, chat, and email access to Cloud Support Engineers, full Trusted Advisor checks, recommended for production workloads), Enterprise On-Ramp (a Technical Account Manager (TAM) pool, 30-minute response on business-critical incidents, suitable for the early production stage), and Enterprise (a designated TAM, 15-minute response on business-critical incidents, Concierge Support team for billing, Infrastructure Event Management, Well-Architected Reviews, and Operations Reviews). Only Business, Enterprise On-Ramp, and Enterprise plans grant full Trusted Advisor and 24x7 access to Cloud Support Engineers. Only Enterprise On-Ramp and Enterprise grant access to a Technical Account Manager. AWS Trust and Safety is the team to report abuse such as spam, port scanning, or DDoS originating from AWS resources.

Domain 4: AWS technical resources, partners, and Marketplace. AWS publishes documentation, whitepapers, and reference architectures on aws.amazon.com, with key destinations including the AWS Knowledge Center (answers to the most frequent questions), AWS re:Post (community Q&A that replaced AWS Forums), AWS Prescriptive Guidance (proven patterns and anti-patterns), and the AWS Security Blog. The AWS Partner Network (APN) includes Consulting Partners (system integrators that build and operate on AWS) and Technology Partners (independent software vendors whose products run on or with AWS), and partners earn tiers (Select, Advanced, Premier) based on certifications and customer references. AWS Marketplace is a curated digital catalog where customers can find, buy, and deploy third-party software, including AMIs, SaaS, and container images, with consolidated billing through their AWS account and options like governance and entitlement management. AWS Professional Services is the AWS internal consulting organization that helps customers achieve specific outcomes; AWS Solutions Architects help with architecture guidance; AWS IQ connects customers with certified third-party experts. The AWS Health Dashboard provides personalized service health information and the AWS Health API enables programmatic access to events that may impact your account.

flashcards.front: Define elasticity in the AWS context and contrast it with scalability.
flashcards.back: Scalability is the ability to grow capacity to handle increased load (often by adding instances). Elasticity goes further: capacity scales both up AND down automatically with demand using AWS Auto Scaling, so you only pay for what you need at a given moment. Elasticity = scalability + automated rightsizing in real time.
flashcards.hint: One word starts with E and includes shrinking, not just growing.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a Region, an Availability Zone, and an edge location?
flashcards.back: Region: a separate geographic area (e.g. us-east-1) made of multiple AZs. AZ: one or more discrete data centers with redundant power, networking, and connectivity, physically separated within a Region. Edge location: a CloudFront/Global Accelerator point of presence used to cache content close to end users, far more numerous than Regions or AZs.
flashcards.hint: Think country → city → corner store.

flashcards.front: List the six pillars of the AWS Well-Architected Framework.
flashcards.back: Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, and Sustainability. Sustainability was added in 2021. Note these are pillars (not 'principles' or 'standards') — the exam often uses 'pillars' as a precise term.
flashcards.hint: Six fellowship members of the well-architected scroll.

flashcards.front: What does CapEx vs OpEx mean for cloud economics?
flashcards.back: CapEx (capital expenditure) is upfront spend on physical assets like servers — characteristic of on-premises. OpEx (operating expenditure) is ongoing pay-as-you-go spend on services consumed. AWS converts CapEx to OpEx, removing forecasting risk and freeing capital for the business.
flashcards.hint: One you write off over years; the other you pay this month.

flashcards.front: What are the seven Rs of cloud migration?
flashcards.back: Retire, Retain, Rehost (lift-and-shift), Relocate (move VMs without conversion via VMware Cloud on AWS), Replatform (lift-tinker-shift), Repurchase (drop-and-shop / SaaS), and Refactor (re-architect cloud-native). The original AWS list had 6 Rs; Relocate was added when VMware Cloud on AWS made it a distinct option.
flashcards.hint: All begin with R, all describe what to do with a workload.

flashcards.front: What is the AWS Cloud Adoption Framework (CAF)?
flashcards.back: AWS CAF organizes cloud transformation across six perspectives: Business, People, Governance, Platform, Security, and Operations. It also identifies expected outcomes such as reduced business risk, improved ESG performance, increased revenue, and improved operational efficiency. CAF is a planning framework — not a deployment service.
flashcards.hint: Think 'how does an organization adopt AWS', not 'how do I deploy a Lambda'.

flashcards.front: What is rightsizing and which AWS service makes recommendations for it?
flashcards.back: Rightsizing means continuously matching instance type and size to actual workload demand to avoid over- or under-provisioning. AWS Compute Optimizer analyzes utilization and recommends optimal EC2, EBS, Lambda, and Auto Scaling configurations.
flashcards.hint: Tailor the cloth to the customer.

flashcards.front: Why deploy across multiple Availability Zones (AZs)?
flashcards.back: AZs are physically isolated and have independent power, cooling, and networking, so a fault in one AZ should not cascade. Deploying across multiple AZs provides high availability inside a Region. Single-AZ deployments cannot survive an AZ-level outage.
flashcards.hint: Don't keep all your eggs in one data center.

flashcards.front: When would you use multiple Regions instead of multi-AZ?
flashcards.back: Use multi-Region for disaster recovery and business continuity (Region-level outage), low latency for globally distributed users, and data sovereignty/residency requirements that mandate data stay in a specific country or jurisdiction. Multi-Region is more complex and costly than multi-AZ — only use it when one of those drivers applies.
flashcards.hint: DR, latency, sovereignty.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between high availability and fault tolerance?
flashcards.back: High availability (HA) means the workload remains operational despite component failures, possibly with brief degraded performance during failover. Fault tolerance is stronger: the workload continues with no perceptible degradation, often via active-active redundancy. Multi-AZ RDS = HA; an active-active multi-Region design with no measurable interruption = fault tolerance.
flashcards.hint: HA = recover quickly; FT = never miss a beat.

flashcards.front: What are economies of scale in the AWS context?
flashcards.back: Because AWS aggregates demand from millions of customers, it negotiates lower prices for hardware, power, and connectivity than any single company could. AWS passes some of these savings to customers in the form of regular price reductions. Customers benefit by paying less per compute hour or per gigabyte than they could achieve on-premises.
flashcards.hint: The bigger the order, the cheaper each unit.

flashcards.front: What does BYOL mean and when does it apply?
flashcards.back: BYOL = Bring Your Own License. Customers reuse their existing software licenses (Microsoft Windows Server, SQL Server, Oracle, etc.) on AWS instead of paying for license-included pricing. BYOL typically requires Dedicated Hosts (or Dedicated Instances) for compliance and license tracking. License-included pricing bundles the license cost into hourly service rates such as RDS for SQL Server.
flashcards.hint: Bring the key you already paid for.

flashcards.front: How does the cloud reduce TCO compared with on-premises?
flashcards.back: On-premises TCO includes hardware purchase + refresh, real estate, power and cooling, networking, security staffing, and software licenses. Cloud removes most of those line items by pricing them into the service. Customers also avoid over-provisioning (no idle capacity bought 'just in case') because elasticity scales to actual demand.
flashcards.hint: Add up everything a server room costs, then strike most of it out.

flashcards.front: Which Well-Architected pillar covers least-privilege IAM and encryption at rest?
flashcards.back: Security. Operational Excellence covers operations and observability, Reliability covers recovery and resilience, Performance Efficiency covers efficient use of compute, Cost Optimization covers spend, and Sustainability covers environmental impact. Encryption and least-privilege IAM are textbook Security pillar examples.
flashcards.hint: Pillar #2 in the standard ordering.

flashcards.front: Which Well-Architected pillar covers right-sizing instances and using Reserved Instances?
flashcards.back: Cost Optimization. This pillar's design principles include: implement cloud financial management, adopt a consumption model, measure overall efficiency, stop spending money on undifferentiated heavy lifting, and analyze and attribute expenditure.
flashcards.hint: If the answer is 'spend less, get the same outcome', it's this pillar.

flashcards.front: Which Well-Architected pillar was added most recently and what does it cover?
flashcards.back: Sustainability — added in 2021. It focuses on minimizing the environmental impact of cloud workloads by maximizing utilization, choosing efficient hardware, using managed services, and selecting Regions powered by lower-carbon energy.
flashcards.hint: Green pillar, youngest of the six.

flashcards.front: What is the AWS Snow Family used for?
flashcards.back: Snow Family is physical edge computing and data transfer hardware: Snowcone (small, ~8 TB) and Snowball Edge (~80 TB Storage Optimized or compute-heavy variants). It transfers very large datasets (TB to PB) into AWS faster and cheaper than network transfer when bandwidth is limited.
flashcards.hint: When the network is too slow, send a shipping crate instead.

flashcards.front: What does 'agility' mean in the cloud value proposition?
flashcards.back: Agility = how quickly an organization can experiment, deliver, and respond to change. The cloud delivers agility through self-service provisioning, infrastructure as code, managed services that remove undifferentiated work, and fast global rollout. New environments take minutes instead of weeks of procurement and racking.
flashcards.hint: Speed of change, not amount of stuff.

flashcards.front: Lift-and-shift vs replatform vs refactor — what are they?
flashcards.back: Lift-and-shift (Rehost): move the workload onto EC2 with no code changes — fastest, least benefit. Replatform (lift-tinker-shift): small targeted optimizations like moving SQL Server to RDS but keeping app code. Refactor: re-architect into cloud-native services like Lambda, DynamoDB, EventBridge — slowest, biggest long-term win.
flashcards.hint: Three Rs on a spectrum from 'box it up' to 'rebuild it'.

flashcards.front: What is the AWS Well-Architected Tool?
flashcards.back: A free tool in the AWS Management Console that walks through Well-Architected questions for a workload, identifies high- and medium-risk issues against the six pillars, and produces an improvement plan. Distinct from the Framework itself, which is the underlying body of best practices.
flashcards.hint: The Framework is the doctrine; the Tool is the questionnaire.

flashcards.front: State the AWS shared responsibility model in one sentence.
flashcards.back: AWS is responsible for security OF the cloud (hardware, hypervisor, physical data centers, global network); customers are responsible for security IN the cloud (their data, IAM, OS patches on EC2, network configuration, encryption choices, application code).
flashcards.hint: Two prepositions: OF and IN.

flashcards.front: On Amazon RDS, who patches the database engine?
flashcards.back: AWS does — RDS is a managed service, so AWS patches the host OS and the database engine during configurable maintenance windows. The customer is still responsible for users, schemas, IAM access, encryption choices, and choosing to enable Multi-AZ.
flashcards.hint: If a service is 'managed', AWS owns more of the stack.

flashcards.front: On Amazon EC2, who patches the operating system?
flashcards.back: The customer does. EC2 is Infrastructure-as-a-Service: AWS provides the hypervisor and hardware, but the guest OS, applications, and any installed software are the customer's responsibility. AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager helps automate the process.
flashcards.hint: EC2 = bring your own OS hygiene.

flashcards.front: Which AWS service detects threats by analyzing CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs?
flashcards.back: Amazon GuardDuty. It uses machine learning and threat-intel feeds to spot suspicious activity such as cryptocurrency mining, port-scanning, or compromised credentials. No agent installation required.
flashcards.hint: A watchdog with a Greek name.

flashcards.front: Which AWS service automatically discovers and classifies sensitive data (PII/PHI) in S3?
flashcards.back: Amazon Macie. It uses ML to find personally identifiable information, financial data, and credentials in S3 buckets and reports findings (such as publicly accessible buckets containing PII).
flashcards.hint: French-sounding name; works on S3.

flashcards.front: Which AWS service performs vulnerability scanning on EC2, ECR images, and Lambda?
flashcards.back: Amazon Inspector. It continuously scans for software vulnerabilities (CVEs) and unintended network exposure, scoring findings by severity. Replaces the older agent-based Inspector v1.
flashcards.hint: It... inspects.

flashcards.front: What is AWS Security Hub?
flashcards.back: A central dashboard that aggregates security findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, IAM Access Analyzer, AWS partners, and more. It also runs continuous compliance checks against standards like CIS AWS Foundations, PCI DSS, and AWS Foundational Security Best Practices.
flashcards.hint: Single pane of glass for findings.

flashcards.front: What is AWS CloudTrail and what does it record?
flashcards.back: CloudTrail records every AWS API call made in your account — who, what, when, from where — and delivers logs to S3 for governance, audit, and compliance. Management events and data events can be logged. CloudTrail is on by default for the last 90 days of management events viewable in the Event History.
flashcards.hint: Audit trail of every API click and CLI call.

flashcards.front: What is AWS Config and how does it differ from CloudTrail?
flashcards.back: AWS Config records configuration state and changes for AWS resources over time and evaluates them against rules (e.g. 'all EBS volumes must be encrypted'). CloudTrail records who did what (API calls); Config records what the resource looks like and whether it's compliant. They complement each other.
flashcards.hint: CloudTrail = actions; Config = state.

flashcards.front: Which AWS service is the central portal for compliance reports (SOC, ISO, PCI, HIPAA BAA)?
flashcards.back: AWS Artifact. It provides on-demand access to AWS's compliance documentation and lets customers accept agreements like the HIPAA Business Associate Addendum (BAA).
flashcards.hint: Where you go to download the audit PDFs.

flashcards.front: What is the principle of least privilege?
flashcards.back: Grant only the minimum permissions needed to perform a task — no wildcards, no unused permissions. Reduces blast radius if credentials are compromised. Implemented in IAM through narrow Action and Resource specifiers and Condition keys.
flashcards.hint: If you don't need it, you don't get it.

flashcards.front: List five tasks that ONLY the AWS root user can perform.
flashcards.back: Change the account name, email address, or root password. Close the AWS account. Change the support plan. Restore IAM user permissions if a misconfiguration locked the account out. Register as a seller in the Reserved Instance Marketplace.
flashcards.hint: Things tied to ownership of the account itself.

flashcards.front: Why use IAM roles instead of IAM users for EC2 workloads?
flashcards.back: IAM roles use temporary credentials issued by STS, automatically rotated, and never embedded in code or AMIs. IAM users carry long-lived access keys that can leak in source control or backups. EC2 instance profiles attach roles directly to instances so applications use the SDK with no key management at all.
flashcards.hint: No baked-in credentials = no credential leaks.

flashcards.front: What is AWS IAM Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO)?
flashcards.back: A workforce-identity service that gives users single sign-on access to multiple AWS accounts and business applications, integrating with external IdPs like Active Directory, Okta, or Azure AD via SAML/SCIM. It centralizes lifecycle and permission set management across an Organization.
flashcards.hint: One login, many AWS accounts.

flashcards.front: What is Amazon Cognito and how is it different from IAM Identity Center?
flashcards.back: Cognito is for application end-user identity — sign-up, sign-in, social and enterprise federation for web/mobile apps. IAM Identity Center is for workforce (employee) identity accessing AWS accounts. They solve different problems: customer-facing vs internal-facing.
flashcards.hint: App users vs AWS users.

flashcards.front: AWS KMS vs AWS CloudHSM — when to use each?
flashcards.back: KMS is a multi-tenant managed key service that's cheap, integrates with most AWS services, and is FIPS 140-2 Level 2 (some keys Level 3). CloudHSM is a single-tenant dedicated FIPS 140-2 Level 3 hardware security module — pick CloudHSM when regulations require dedicated hardware or you need direct PKCS#11/JCE integration. Default to KMS unless you have a hard reason not to.
flashcards.hint: Default to KMS; reach for CloudHSM only when forced.

flashcards.front: What is AWS Secrets Manager?
flashcards.back: A service that stores, retrieves, and automatically rotates secrets like database credentials, API keys, and OAuth tokens. It can rotate RDS credentials with a Lambda rotation function. Distinct from SSM Parameter Store (cheaper, simpler, no automatic rotation, used for configuration).
flashcards.hint: Vault for credentials with auto-rotation.

flashcards.front: What is AWS WAF?
flashcards.back: Web Application Firewall — inspects HTTP/HTTPS requests and blocks SQL injection, XSS, malicious bots, and other layer-7 attacks using customer-defined or AWS-managed rule groups. Attaches to CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, AppSync, and Cognito user pools.
flashcards.hint: Layer 7 web filter.

flashcards.front: AWS Shield Standard vs Shield Advanced — what's the difference?
flashcards.back: Shield Standard: free, automatic, defends against common L3/L4 DDoS for all AWS customers. Shield Advanced: paid, adds higher-tier protection, 24x7 access to the Shield Response Team (SRT), DDoS cost protection (refunds for scaling charges during an attack), and tighter integration with WAF and Route 53.
flashcards.hint: Free shield for everyone, paid shield for the paranoid.

flashcards.front: Security group vs network ACL — what's the difference?
flashcards.back: Security group: stateful (return traffic auto-allowed), instance-level (attached to ENIs), allow rules only. Network ACL (NACL): stateless (must allow both directions), subnet-level, supports allow AND deny rules, evaluated by rule number. SGs are first-line filtering; NACLs are coarse subnet-wide guardrails.
flashcards.hint: Stateful & instance-level vs stateless & subnet-level.

flashcards.front: What is AWS Audit Manager?
flashcards.back: A service that continuously collects evidence from AWS usage and maps it to control frameworks like HIPAA, GDPR, PCI DSS, and SOC 2 to streamline audit prep. Reduces the manual evidence-gathering burden that auditors typically require.
flashcards.hint: Evidence-collector for compliance audits.

flashcards.front: What is Amazon Detective?
flashcards.back: A service that ingests CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and GuardDuty findings, builds a behavior graph, and helps security teams investigate the root cause of suspicious activity. Where GuardDuty alerts you, Detective helps you investigate the alert.
flashcards.hint: GuardDuty raises the alarm; Detective figures out who did it.

flashcards.front: What is AWS Firewall Manager?
flashcards.back: A central management service for configuring WAF rules, Shield Advanced protections, security group baselines, and AWS Network Firewall policies across an entire AWS Organization. Useful when many accounts must enforce consistent network security.
flashcards.hint: WAF/Shield/SG governance across an Org.

flashcards.front: Encryption in transit vs encryption at rest — give an example of each.
flashcards.back: Encryption in transit: TLS between a user's browser and CloudFront, or between an app and Aurora. Encryption at rest: SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS encrypting an S3 object on disk; EBS volume encryption; RDS storage encryption with KMS. Both should be enabled for sensitive workloads.
flashcards.hint: Moving data vs sleeping data.

flashcards.front: Compare the four ways to access AWS: Console, CLI, SDK, IaC.
flashcards.back: Console: GUI for ad hoc and learning. CLI (aws ...): scripted commands from a terminal. SDK: programmatic access from app code (Python boto3, JS, Java, Go, etc.). IaC: declarative templates like AWS CloudFormation or AWS CDK that version control and repeat deployments. Use Console for exploration, CLI/SDK for scripts, IaC for production/repeatable infra.
flashcards.hint: Eyes, fingers, code, manifests.

flashcards.front: Name the five EC2 instance type families and their primary use case.
flashcards.back: General Purpose (T, M): balanced CPU/RAM, web servers and small DBs. Compute Optimized (C): high CPU per dollar, batch processing and HPC. Memory Optimized (R, X): high RAM per dollar, large in-memory DBs and SAP. Storage Optimized (D, I, H): high local NVMe IOPS, NoSQL and data warehouses. Accelerated (P, G, Inf, Trn): GPUs and ML accelerators for training/inference and graphics.
flashcards.hint: TM/C/R-X/D-I/P-G — general/compute/memory/storage/accelerated.

flashcards.front: What is AWS Lambda?
flashcards.back: A serverless compute service that runs code in response to events with no servers to manage. Pay per request and per GB-second of execution. Max execution duration 15 minutes per invocation. Triggers include API Gateway, S3, EventBridge, SQS, DynamoDB Streams, and many more. Canonical 'serverless' compute on AWS.
flashcards.hint: Event happens → function fires → bill is in fractions of a cent.

flashcards.front: AWS Fargate vs EC2 launch type for ECS/EKS — what's the difference?
flashcards.back: Fargate: AWS provisions and manages the underlying infrastructure for your containers; you specify CPU/memory and pay per vCPU-second and GB-second. No host patching, no capacity planning. EC2 launch type: you manage the EC2 cluster yourself, more control and potentially cheaper at large scale, but more operational overhead. Fargate is the serverless container option.
flashcards.hint: Fargate = forget about the host.

flashcards.front: ECS vs EKS — when to use each?
flashcards.back: ECS: AWS-native container orchestrator, simpler, deep AWS integration, good if you don't need Kubernetes. EKS: managed Kubernetes, the choice if you need k8s portability, existing k8s tooling, or multi-cloud strategy. Both can run on Fargate or EC2.
flashcards.hint: Native AWS vs portable Kubernetes.

flashcards.front: What is AWS Elastic Beanstalk?
flashcards.back: A platform-as-a-service (PaaS) that takes uploaded code (Java, .NET, PHP, Node.js, Python, Ruby, Go, Docker) and provisions EC2, ELB, Auto Scaling, and CloudWatch automatically. You manage the application; AWS manages the platform. Cheaper alternative to building everything yourself, less control than raw EC2.
flashcards.hint: Upload code → AWS handles the runway.

flashcards.front: What is Amazon Lightsail used for?
flashcards.back: Simple, predictable monthly-priced bundles of VMs, storage, networking, and managed databases — designed for small workloads, learning, and quick deployments. Good for blogs, dev/test environments, and small business sites; not for production at scale where EC2 + ELB + Auto Scaling is more appropriate.
flashcards.hint: AWS for people who don't want to think about AWS yet.

flashcards.front: What is AWS Auto Scaling?
flashcards.back: A service that automatically adjusts the number of EC2 instances (or ECS tasks, DynamoDB capacity, etc.) based on demand using policies like target tracking, step scaling, scheduled scaling, or predictive scaling. The mechanism behind AWS elasticity for compute.
flashcards.hint: Demand goes up, fleet grows; demand drops, fleet shrinks.

flashcards.front: ALB vs NLB vs GWLB — pick a layer-7 vs layer-4 vs gateway use case.
flashcards.back: Application Load Balancer (ALB): layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS), path-based and host-based routing, ideal for web apps and microservices. Network Load Balancer (NLB): layer 4 (TCP/UDP/TLS), extreme performance, static IPs, ideal for high-throughput, low-latency, or non-HTTP traffic. Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB): layer 3, deploys, scales, and manages third-party network appliances like firewalls and IDS/IPS.
flashcards.hint: Apps, packets, appliances.

flashcards.front: What is Amazon RDS Multi-AZ?
flashcards.back: A high-availability deployment option where RDS provisions a synchronous standby replica in a different AZ. On primary failure, RDS automatically fails over to the standby (typically <2 minutes) without changing the DNS endpoint. Multi-AZ is for HA, not read scaling — read replicas are for read scaling.
flashcards.hint: Synchronous standby for failover, not scaling.

flashcards.front: What is Amazon Aurora and what makes it different from RDS for MySQL?
flashcards.back: Aurora is AWS's MySQL- and PostgreSQL-compatible database engine with up to 5x MySQL throughput. Its storage layer is fault-tolerant and self-healing, replicating six copies across three AZs. Aurora supports up to 15 read replicas with sub-10ms replica lag. More expensive than RDS for MySQL but far higher performance and durability.
flashcards.hint: Cloud-native rewrite of MySQL/PostgreSQL by AWS.

flashcards.front: What is Amazon DynamoDB?
flashcards.back: Fully managed serverless NoSQL key-value and document database with single-digit millisecond latency at any scale. Features: encryption at rest, point-in-time recovery, global tables for multi-Region replication, DAX for in-memory acceleration, on-demand or provisioned capacity. No instance to size.
flashcards.hint: Serverless NoSQL — no shards to manage.

flashcards.front: What is Amazon ElastiCache and what engines does it support?
flashcards.back: Managed in-memory caching with two engines: Redis OSS (and Valkey) for rich data structures, replication, and persistence, and Memcached for simple multi-threaded key-value. Used to reduce DB load and accelerate read-heavy workloads.
flashcards.hint: Sits in front of your DB and serves hot data.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between AWS DMS and AWS SCT?
flashcards.back: AWS Database Migration Service (DMS): moves DATA between sources and targets with minimal downtime, including ongoing replication. AWS Schema Conversion Tool (SCT): converts SCHEMA (and stored procedures) between heterogeneous engines, e.g. Oracle → Aurora PostgreSQL or SQL Server → MySQL. SCT first to convert structure; DMS to move the data.
flashcards.hint: SCT does shape; DMS does substance.

flashcards.front: List the main components of an Amazon VPC.
flashcards.back: CIDR block (IPv4/IPv6), subnets (one per AZ), route tables, internet gateway, NAT gateway, virtual private gateway (for VPN), security groups, network ACLs, VPC endpoints (gateway and interface), Elastic IPs, and ENIs (elastic network interfaces).
flashcards.hint: Network primitives in your private cloud.

flashcards.front: Public subnet vs private subnet — what makes the difference?
flashcards.back: A subnet is 'public' when its route table has a default route (0.0.0.0/0) to an Internet Gateway. A 'private' subnet has no IGW route — outbound internet access goes through a NAT Gateway in a public subnet. Resources in private subnets cannot be reached directly from the internet.
flashcards.hint: Route to IGW = public; route to NAT = private with outbound only.

flashcards.front: What is Amazon Route 53?
flashcards.back: AWS's authoritative DNS service. Supports health checks and routing policies including simple, weighted, latency, geolocation, geoproximity, failover, and multi-value answer. Also a domain registrar. Used for global traffic management and DNS-based DR.
flashcards.hint: DNS named after port 53.

flashcards.front: AWS Direct Connect vs Site-to-Site VPN — when to choose each?
flashcards.back: Direct Connect: dedicated private fiber connection between an on-prem data center and AWS, consistent bandwidth (1/10/100 Gbps), low jitter, but takes weeks to provision. VPN: encrypted tunnel over the public internet, set up in minutes, lower cost, but bandwidth and latency depend on the internet. Use VPN as a quick backup for Direct Connect.
flashcards.hint: Wired private line vs encrypted tunnel.

flashcards.front: What is Amazon CloudFront?
flashcards.back: A global content delivery network (CDN) that caches content at hundreds of edge locations to reduce latency and offload origins like S3, ALB, or any HTTP server. Integrates with AWS WAF, Shield, ACM (free TLS certs), and Lambda@Edge / CloudFront Functions for edge compute.
flashcards.hint: AWS's CDN — pushes content to the edge.

flashcards.front: What is AWS Global Accelerator and how is it different from CloudFront?
flashcards.back: Global Accelerator routes TCP/UDP traffic over the AWS global network from the nearest edge location to the optimal application endpoint, providing two static anycast IPs. Best for non-HTTP workloads, gaming, IoT, or whenever you need static IPs. CloudFront caches HTTP/HTTPS content; Global Accelerator does not cache.
flashcards.hint: Network-layer accelerator vs HTTP cache.

flashcards.front: List S3 storage classes from most-frequently accessed to deepest archive.
flashcards.back: S3 Standard → S3 Intelligent-Tiering → S3 Standard-IA → S3 One Zone-IA → S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval → S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval → S3 Glacier Deep Archive. Lifecycle policies move objects across these classes based on age or access patterns to reduce cost.
flashcards.hint: Hot → warm → cold → frozen → buried.

flashcards.front: S3 vs EBS vs EFS — when to use each?
flashcards.back: S3: object storage, internet-accessible, virtually unlimited, durable across AZs — for static assets, backups, data lakes, media. EBS: block storage attached to a single EC2 in a single AZ — for OS disks, databases needing low latency. EFS: NFS file system, shared by many EC2s and Lambda across AZs — for shared application files and content management.
flashcards.hint: Objects via API, blocks per instance, files shared by many.

flashcards.front: What is AWS Storage Gateway used for?
flashcards.back: Hybrid storage. File Gateway gives on-prem servers an SMB/NFS interface backed by S3. Volume Gateway presents iSCSI block volumes backed by S3 with EBS snapshots. Tape Gateway emulates a virtual tape library backed by S3 Glacier for backup software. The bridge between on-prem apps and AWS storage.
flashcards.hint: On-prem appliance fronting AWS storage.

flashcards.front: What is AWS Backup?
flashcards.back: A central service to define backup policies (frequency, retention, lifecycle to cold storage, cross-Region copy) and apply them across many AWS services in one place: EBS, RDS, DynamoDB, EFS, FSx, Storage Gateway, EC2, and more. Replaces per-service backup configuration.
flashcards.hint: One pane to govern backups across services.

flashcards.front: Match service to job: real-time chat, image labeling, speech-to-text, doc OCR, translation.
flashcards.back: Real-time chatbot → Amazon Lex. Image/video labels → Amazon Rekognition. Speech-to-text → Amazon Transcribe. Document OCR/forms → Amazon Textract. Translation → Amazon Translate. (Bonus: Polly = text-to-speech, Comprehend = NLP/sentiment, Kendra = enterprise search, Q = generative AI assistant.)
flashcards.hint: Each AI service has a verb in its job.

flashcards.front: What is Amazon Athena?
flashcards.back: A serverless interactive query service that runs SQL directly against data in S3 (CSV, JSON, Parquet, ORC). Pay per terabyte scanned. No infrastructure to manage. Often paired with AWS Glue Data Catalog for schema management. Cheapest way to run ad hoc SQL on S3 data.
flashcards.hint: SQL on S3, no servers.

flashcards.front: SQS vs SNS vs EventBridge — pick one per use case.
flashcards.back: SQS: queue, one consumer pulls each message, decouple producers and consumers. SNS: pub/sub topic, one message fanned out to many subscribers (SQS, Lambda, HTTP, email/SMS). EventBridge: serverless event bus with content-based filtering and SaaS partner sources, modern successor to CloudWatch Events. Use SQS to buffer work, SNS to fan out, EventBridge for cross-service event-driven architectures.
flashcards.hint: Queue, broadcast, smart router.

flashcards.front: What is AWS Step Functions?
flashcards.back: A serverless workflow orchestrator that chains Lambda functions and other AWS services into visual state machines using JSON-based Amazon States Language. Handles retries, error handling, parallel branches, and human-approval steps. Great for long-running business processes or data pipelines.
flashcards.hint: Flowchart for cloud workflows.

flashcards.front: What are Amazon WorkSpaces and AppStream 2.0?
flashcards.back: WorkSpaces: managed virtual desktops (Windows/Linux) streamed to end users. Persistent user environment, replaces traditional VDI. AppStream 2.0: streams individual applications (not whole desktops) from AWS to a browser, ideal for granular app delivery. Both are end-user computing services.
flashcards.hint: Desktop in the cloud vs single app in the cloud.

flashcards.front: What is Amazon Redshift used for?
flashcards.back: A petabyte-scale managed data warehouse for analytics with massively parallel processing (MPP) columnar storage. Handles complex SQL analytics over very large datasets. Redshift Spectrum extends queries to data in S3. Distinct from RDS (OLTP) and Athena (serverless on-S3 SQL).
flashcards.hint: Data warehouse for big analytics.

flashcards.front: What is Amazon EventBridge?
flashcards.back: A serverless event bus that routes events from AWS services, SaaS partners (Datadog, Zendesk, etc.), and custom applications to targets like Lambda, SQS, Step Functions using rules with content-based filtering. Modern successor to CloudWatch Events.
flashcards.hint: Smart router for events across the cloud.

flashcards.front: Compare On-Demand, Reserved, Spot, and Savings Plans for EC2.
flashcards.back: On-Demand: pay per second/hour, no commitment, best for short or unpredictable workloads. Reserved Instances (1y/3y, Standard or Convertible): up to ~72% off for committed steady-state workloads, tied to instance attributes. Spot: up to ~90% off using spare capacity, can be reclaimed in 2 minutes — fault-tolerant batch only. Savings Plans (Compute or EC2 Instance): commit to $/hour for 1 or 3 years, more flexible than RIs (Compute SP also covers Fargate and Lambda).
flashcards.hint: Pay full, commit & save, scavenge spare, or pledge a budget.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a Compute Savings Plan and an EC2 Instance Savings Plan?
flashcards.back: Compute Savings Plan: up to 66% off, applies to EC2 (any family, any Region, any OS, any tenancy), Fargate, and Lambda — most flexible. EC2 Instance Savings Plan: up to 72% off, locked to a specific EC2 family in a specific Region (OS and tenancy can vary) — biggest discount but least flexible.
flashcards.hint: More discount = less flexibility.

flashcards.front: When are Spot Instances appropriate and when are they not?
flashcards.back: Use Spot for fault-tolerant, flexible workloads: batch processing, big data, CI/CD runners, stateless web tier with autoscaling fallback to On-Demand. Don't use Spot for stateful databases, long-running jobs that can't checkpoint, or anything where a 2-minute reclamation notice would cause data loss.
flashcards.hint: If a 2-minute warning would ruin you, use On-Demand.

flashcards.front: What's the difference between Dedicated Hosts and Dedicated Instances?
flashcards.back: Dedicated Host: a physical server dedicated to your account, you control instance placement on the host, exposes physical socket and core counts, supports BYOL for license-bound software. Dedicated Instance: instance runs on hardware dedicated to your account but you don't control the underlying host. Hosts are for license compliance/visibility; Dedicated Instances are for isolation only.
flashcards.hint: One reveals the metal; one only isolates it.

flashcards.front: Inbound vs outbound data transfer pricing on AWS — what's the rule of thumb?
flashcards.back: Inbound (data into AWS) is free in most cases. Outbound (data out to the internet) is charged per GB and varies by Region. Within a Region: traffic between AZs using private IPs costs a small amount; same-AZ private IP traffic is free. Cross-Region traffic incurs charges. CloudFront and Direct Connect typically lower egress costs.
flashcards.hint: Inbound free, outbound costs, cross-AZ small, cross-Region more.

flashcards.front: What does AWS Organizations consolidated billing do?
flashcards.back: Combines billing for many AWS accounts under one management account, producing one invoice. Volume tiered pricing applies across the combined usage (cheaper at scale). Reserved Instance and Savings Plans benefits can be shared across accounts. Each member account remains independent for resources and IAM.
flashcards.hint: Many accounts, one bill, shared discounts.

flashcards.front: What is AWS Cost Explorer?
flashcards.back: A console UI that visualizes and analyzes AWS costs and usage over time. Shows trends, forecasts, and recommendations for Reserved Instances and Savings Plans. Supports filtering by service, account, tag, Region, and more. Free to use.
flashcards.hint: Charts and forecasts of your AWS spend.

flashcards.front: What is AWS Budgets used for?
flashcards.back: Setting custom cost or usage budgets and triggering alerts via SNS or email when actual or forecasted spend exceeds thresholds. Can also trigger actions (e.g. apply an IAM policy or stop EC2 instances) when a budget is breached. Proactive — Cost Explorer is reactive.
flashcards.hint: Set the limit, get pinged when it's hit.

flashcards.front: What is the AWS Cost and Usage Report (CUR)?
flashcards.back: The most detailed billing dataset AWS publishes, delivered to S3 with hourly granularity and every line item. Queryable from Athena, Redshift, or QuickSight. Used for chargeback, showback, and detailed cost analysis. Cost Explorer summarizes; CUR is the raw data.
flashcards.hint: Granular receipts, in S3, queried with SQL.

flashcards.front: What are cost allocation tags?
flashcards.back: Key/value tags applied to AWS resources that, once activated in the Billing console, allow cost reporting broken down by application, project, environment, owner, or any other dimension. Both AWS-generated tags (e.g. aws:createdBy) and user-defined tags must be activated explicitly before they appear in CUR/Cost Explorer.
flashcards.hint: Label your resources, slice your bill.

flashcards.front: List the five AWS Support plans from cheapest to most expensive.
flashcards.back: Basic (free) → Developer → Business → Enterprise On-Ramp → Enterprise. Basic gives only account/billing support and core Trusted Advisor. Developer adds business-hours email. Business adds 24x7 phone/chat, full Trusted Advisor. Enterprise On-Ramp adds a TAM pool and 30-min response on critical issues. Enterprise adds a designated TAM and 15-min response.
flashcards.hint: Five plans, two with TAMs.

flashcards.front: Which AWS Support plans include access to a Technical Account Manager (TAM)?
flashcards.back: Enterprise On-Ramp (TAM pool, shared across customers) and Enterprise (designated TAM, dedicated to your account). Business plan does NOT include a TAM. Distinguishing TAM access is a frequent CLF-C02 question.
flashcards.hint: Only the top two tiers.

flashcards.front: What are the five categories of AWS Trusted Advisor checks?
flashcards.back: Cost Optimization, Performance, Security, Fault Tolerance, and Service Limits (now Service Quotas). Basic and Developer plans get a limited subset; Business, Enterprise On-Ramp, and Enterprise get all checks.
flashcards.hint: Five-pointed star of cloud hygiene.

flashcards.front: What is the AWS Health Dashboard?
flashcards.back: A personalized view of the health of AWS services and your specific resources, including planned maintenance and ongoing incidents. Replaces the old AWS Personal Health Dashboard and Service Health Dashboard. The AWS Health API provides programmatic access to events.
flashcards.hint: Status page tailored to your account.

flashcards.front: What is AWS Marketplace?
flashcards.back: A digital catalog where customers find, buy, and deploy third-party software (AMIs, SaaS, container images, ML models, data) with billing consolidated through their AWS account. Includes governance and entitlement features. Sellers reach AWS customers; buyers get one bill and centralized procurement.
flashcards.hint: App store for AWS-deployed software.

flashcards.front: AWS Partner Network (APN): what are Consulting Partners vs Technology Partners?
flashcards.back: Consulting Partners: system integrators, MSPs, and resellers that help customers design, migrate, and operate on AWS. Technology Partners: independent software vendors (ISVs) whose products run on or with AWS. Partners earn tiers (Select, Advanced, Premier) based on certifications, revenue, and customer success.
flashcards.hint: Builders vs makers.

flashcards.front: AWS re:Post vs AWS Knowledge Center vs AWS Prescriptive Guidance — what is each?
flashcards.back: re:Post: community Q&A site that replaced AWS Forums. Knowledge Center: official AWS-curated answers to the most frequently asked support questions. Prescriptive Guidance: AWS-published proven patterns, anti-patterns, and migration playbooks. Pick re:Post for community help, Knowledge Center for canonical answers, Prescriptive Guidance for deeper architectural patterns.
flashcards.hint: Community, canon, doctrine.

quiz.question: A startup is launching a consumer mobile app and cannot predict whether it will have 1,000 or 1,000,000 users in the first month. The CTO wants to avoid both stockouts (slow app, lost users) and large upfront server purchases. Which AWS Cloud benefit MOST directly addresses this concern?
options: Elasticity through automatic scaling up and down with demand
quiz.explanation: Brave founder, this is a textbook elasticity scenario. Elasticity scales capacity up AND down with demand automatically (via AWS Auto Scaling), so the startup pays for what it needs at any moment without overbuying. High availability is about surviving failures, not handling unpredictable demand. Global reach is about latency to users. Operational excellence is about how you operate, not how you scale.

quiz.question: A healthcare provider must keep patient data within the European Union due to GDPR data residency requirements. They also need to survive the failure of any single data center without downtime. Which deployment design is BEST?
options: Single AZ in eu-west-1 with daily backups to us-east-1
Multiple Regions globally for maximum redundancy
Multiple Availability Zones within an EU Region (e.g. eu-west-1)
A single AZ in eu-west-1 with a hot standby in eu-west-2
quiz.explanation: Multi-AZ in a single EU Region delivers high availability (survives a data center / AZ failure) while keeping data inside the EU jurisdiction. Multi-Region globally violates data residency. Backups crossing into us-east-1 also violate residency. A single AZ does not survive an AZ failure regardless of where the standby is.

quiz.question: A company is rewriting practices around how it builds and operates AWS workloads. Leadership wants a structured way to evaluate workloads against cloud best practices across security, reliability, performance, cost, operations, and environmental impact. Which AWS resource BEST supports this?
options: AWS Well-Architected Framework and Well-Architected Tool
quiz.explanation: The Well-Architected Framework defines the six pillars (Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, Sustainability) and the Well-Architected Tool walks teams through reviews against those pillars, brave architect. Trusted Advisor offers checks but not the holistic pillar-based review. Config evaluates resource configuration, not architecture practice. Compute Optimizer makes right-sizing recommendations only.

quiz.question: A company is migrating a fleet of legacy Windows applications to AWS. They want the fastest path to running on AWS with the smallest amount of code change, even if that means missing some cloud-native benefits. Which migration strategy is MOST appropriate?
options: Refactor each application into Lambda and DynamoDB
quiz.explanation: Rehost is the fastest, simplest path — move VMs onto EC2 without code changes. Refactor delivers more cloud-native benefit but requires significant rework. Repurchase replaces with SaaS, which may not exist for legacy apps. Retire removes the workload, which is not what the prompt describes.

quiz.question: A financial services firm is comparing the total cost of ownership (TCO) of running workloads on-premises versus on AWS. The CFO wants to understand which on-premises cost categories are typically reduced or eliminated in the cloud. Which list BEST represents these categories?
options: Application source code, employee training, and compliance audits
Data center real estate, power and cooling, hardware refresh, and physical security staffing
Software development, marketing, and customer support
Office supplies, travel, and professional services
quiz.explanation: Cloud economics removes data center real estate, power and cooling, hardware refresh, and physical security operations costs. Software dev, app code, and marketing are not data center costs. Training and audits remain (and may shift in nature) regardless of where workloads run.

quiz.question: A DevOps team wants to provision identical AWS environments for dev, staging, and production with minimal drift between them. They prefer a declarative approach that integrates with version control and code review. Which approach is the BEST fit?
options: Click through the AWS Management Console for each environment
Run a series of AWS CLI commands documented in a wiki page
Use AWS CloudFormation templates checked into a Git repository
Use AWS Trusted Advisor to replicate environments
quiz.explanation: Infrastructure as code (IaC) with CloudFormation is the canonical answer: declarative, version-controllable, code-reviewable, repeatable. The console drifts. CLI scripts in a wiki are not version controlled or declarative. Trusted Advisor doesn't provision anything, brave engineer.

quiz.question: A company runs a global e-commerce site and observes that users in Asia experience high latency loading product images. The team wants to reduce latency without re-architecting the application. Which AWS feature BEST addresses this?
options: Deploying additional Availability Zones in their primary US Region
Caching content at edge locations using Amazon CloudFront
Increasing the size of the EC2 instances hosting the site
Switching from Standard storage to Standard-IA storage
quiz.explanation: CloudFront caches static and dynamic content at hundreds of edge locations close to end users, dramatically reducing latency for global audiences. Adding AZs in the same Region does nothing for users in Asia. Bigger EC2s don't address geographic latency. Storage class changes affect cost, not latency.

quiz.question: A media company must transfer 200 TB of archival video footage from an on-premises NAS to AWS. The data center has a 100 Mbps internet connection. Network transfer is estimated to take months. Which solution is MOST appropriate?
options: Upload the data through a Direct Connect link provisioned for this transfer
Use AWS Snowball Edge devices to ship the data physically
Use AWS DataSync over the existing internet connection
Use S3 Transfer Acceleration with multipart uploads
quiz.explanation: When network bandwidth makes transfer prohibitively slow, Snow Family devices (Snowball Edge here) physically ship the data and complete in days instead of months. Direct Connect takes weeks to provision and is overkill for a one-time transfer. DataSync still bottlenecks at 100 Mbps. Transfer Acceleration helps with global S3 uploads but cannot overcome a slow on-prem pipe.

quiz.question: Availability Zones within an AWS Region share the same physical data center to keep latency between them as low as possible.
quiz.explanation: False. AZs are physically separate data centers with independent power, cooling, and networking, often kilometers apart, but linked with high-bandwidth low-latency networking. The whole point of AZs is that they do not share single points of failure.

quiz.question: A retailer's quarterly Black Friday sale produces a 20x traffic spike for 4 days, then traffic returns to baseline. Year-round, baseline EC2 demand is steady at about 50 instances. Which combination of purchasing options MINIMIZES cost while keeping reliability high?
options: 100% Reserved Instances sized for Black Friday peak
Reserved Instances or Savings Plans for the steady baseline + On-Demand or Spot for the burst
quiz.explanation: Steady predictable load = commit (RIs or Savings Plans) for deepest discount. Burst load = On-Demand (reliable) or Spot (cheap if fault-tolerant). 100% RIs sized for peak overpays the rest of the year. 100% On-Demand overpays on steady-state. 100% Spot risks reclamation during a critical sale.

quiz.question: Which Well-Architected pillar is MOST aligned with the practice of running multiple AZ deployments and automating recovery from component failure?
quiz.explanation: Reliability is the pillar concerned with recovering from failures, meeting demand, and operating consistently. Multi-AZ and automated recovery are core Reliability design principles. Cost Optimization is about spending less. Performance Efficiency is about using compute efficiently. Sustainability is about environmental impact.

quiz.question: Which Well-Architected pillar covers automating operations as code, learning from failures with post-incident reviews, and making frequent small reversible changes?
quiz.explanation: Operational Excellence is about delivering business value through effective operations: IaC, observability, runbooks, frequent small changes, and learning from failures. Security covers protection of data and systems. Reliability covers recovering from failures (operational excellence avoids them in the first place). Cost is unrelated.
