quiz.question: A company wants to know whether moving a workload to AWS will be cheaper than running it on-premises and needs to model out service costs for an architecture that hasn't been deployed yet. Which AWS resource is BEST?
quiz.explanation: Pricing Calculator is for modeling costs of an architecture before it exists, brave planner. Cost Explorer analyzes existing usage. CUR is the detailed billing dataset for actual usage. Budgets sets spending alerts on actual or forecasted spend. Only Pricing Calculator builds 'what if' estimates.

quiz.question: A company is preparing its sustainability report and wants to choose the AWS Region that has the smallest carbon footprint per workload, all else being equal. Which Well-Architected pillar guides this decision MOST directly?
quiz.explanation: The Sustainability pillar (added in 2021) is explicitly about minimizing the environmental impact of cloud workloads, including choosing efficient hardware and Regions powered by lower-carbon energy. Cost Optimization may overlap (efficient = often cheaper) but the question is specifically about carbon footprint.

quiz.question: The AWS Cloud Adoption Framework organizes cloud transformation across six perspectives: Business, People, Governance, Platform, Security, and ___.
quiz.explanation: The six AWS CAF perspectives are Business, People, Governance, Platform, Security, and Operations. Memorize them as: who runs the company (Business), who works there (People), how are decisions enforced (Governance), what tech is used (Platform), is it safe (Security), and how is it run day-to-day (Operations).

quiz.question: A small team needs a single VM with predictable monthly billing to host a low-traffic WordPress blog. They want to avoid configuring VPCs, security groups, and load balancers. Which AWS service is the BEST fit?
options: Amazon EC2 with an Auto Scaling group
quiz.explanation: Lightsail bundles a VM, storage, networking, and managed databases at a flat monthly price with a simplified console — perfect for blogs and small sites. EC2 + Auto Scaling is more powerful but requires VPC config. Beanstalk is for app deployment, not single-VM hosting. Fargate is for containers.

quiz.question: An enterprise CIO wants to reduce risk during cloud adoption by aligning IT, security, finance, and operations teams around a shared transformation plan. Which AWS resource is MOST useful for this organizational planning?
quiz.explanation: AWS CAF is purpose-built for organizational alignment across Business, People, Governance, Platform, Security, and Operations perspectives during cloud adoption. Well-Architected is workload-level technical guidance. Trusted Advisor is operational checks. Service Catalog enforces approved templates. Only CAF tackles the people-and-process side of transformation.

quiz.question: A SaaS company runs a stateless web tier behind an Application Load Balancer. To stop guessing capacity and minimize cost, the team wants instances to grow during the day and shrink overnight automatically. Which combination is the BEST fit?
options: Auto Scaling group with target tracking on average CPU + Application Load Balancer
Single oversized EC2 instance behind a Network Load Balancer
EC2 Spot Fleet with no scaling policy
Reserved Instances sized for peak with no scaling
quiz.explanation: Auto Scaling + ALB is the canonical 'elastic web tier' pattern: target tracking on CPU adds and removes instances to match demand, ALB spreads load and integrates with Auto Scaling health checks. Oversized single instance fails on resilience and overpays. Spot with no policy doesn't address scaling. Peak-sized RIs overpay overnight.

quiz.question: Edge locations are used primarily for running compute-intensive workloads close to end users.
quiz.explanation: False. Edge locations are part of CloudFront and Global Accelerator and are used for caching content (CloudFront) or terminating connections to the AWS global network (Global Accelerator). Compute primarily runs in Availability Zones inside Regions. Lambda@Edge and CloudFront Functions allow some lightweight edge compute but that's not their primary purpose.

quiz.question: A firm currently licenses Microsoft SQL Server with existing perpetual licenses they own. They want to keep using those licenses on AWS to avoid double-paying. Which approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Use license-included pricing on Amazon RDS for SQL Server
Use Bring Your Own License (BYOL) on Dedicated Hosts or Dedicated Instances
Use Amazon Aurora MySQL with the existing SQL Server licenses
Use AWS Lambda with the existing SQL Server licenses
quiz.explanation: BYOL on Dedicated Hosts (or Dedicated Instances depending on the license terms) lets the firm reuse the licenses they already own. License-included would require buying new licenses through AWS. Aurora MySQL doesn't run SQL Server. Lambda is irrelevant.

quiz.question: A new engineer asks which security responsibilities belong to AWS and which belong to the customer when running an application on Amazon EC2. According to the shared responsibility model, who is responsible for patching the GUEST operating system on the EC2 instances?
options: AWS, because EC2 is a managed service
AWS, because the hypervisor isolates guest OSes
The customer, because EC2 is Infrastructure-as-a-Service
AWS Marketplace partners, when the AMI was sourced there
quiz.explanation: EC2 is IaaS — AWS owns the hardware and hypervisor; the customer owns the guest OS, including patching. Brave engineer, the rule of thumb is: more managed = AWS owns more of the stack. EC2 is the least managed and customer-heavy of the compute services.

quiz.question: A company uses Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL. Which of the following remains the customer's responsibility under the shared responsibility model?
options: Patching the underlying host operating system
Replacing failed disks in the RDS storage layer
Configuring database users, IAM access, and encryption choices
quiz.explanation: RDS is managed: AWS handles host OS patching, hardware, and engine patching during maintenance windows. The customer remains responsible for database-level access (users/roles), IAM permissions to call RDS APIs, encryption configuration (KMS CMK choice), and schema/data design.

quiz.question: A startup just opened an AWS account. The CTO asks which security action should be taken FIRST on the new account to protect it.
options: Enable MFA on the root user and avoid using root for daily work
Configure AWS WAF on all Application Load Balancers
quiz.explanation: Protecting the root user is the first action on every new AWS account: enable MFA, store the credentials securely, and create an IAM admin user (or IAM Identity Center user) for daily work. Shield Advanced is for DDoS at scale. WAF requires services to protect. Support tier doesn't secure the account.

quiz.question: A development team needs EC2 instances to read objects from a specific S3 bucket. The team currently embeds an IAM user's access key in an environment variable. Which approach BEST aligns with AWS security best practices?
options: Rotate the IAM user access keys every 90 days
Store the access keys in AWS Secrets Manager and inject at runtime
Attach an IAM role to the EC2 instances with permission to read the S3 bucket
Hard-code the access keys into the AMI used to launch instances
quiz.explanation: IAM roles for EC2 issue temporary credentials via STS, automatically rotated, never embedded in code or AMIs. This eliminates an entire class of credential-leak risk. Rotation, Secrets Manager wrapping, and AMI baking still rely on long-lived keys, brave architect.

quiz.question: A security team wants to ensure that all root user actions across all accounts in the AWS Organization are recorded for audit and review. Which AWS service BEST supports this requirement?
quiz.explanation: CloudTrail records every AWS API call, including root user actions, providing a tamper-evident audit log. Config records resource configuration state, not API calls. CloudWatch Logs is a log destination/aggregation service. Trusted Advisor surfaces best-practice recommendations rather than audit trails.

quiz.question: An auditor requests AWS's SOC 2 Type II report and the signed HIPAA Business Associate Addendum. Where do AWS customers obtain these documents?
quiz.explanation: AWS Artifact is the on-demand portal for AWS's third-party audit reports (SOC, ISO, PCI, FedRAMP) and for accepting agreements like the HIPAA BAA. Trusted Advisor is unrelated. Config evaluates customer resources. Audit Manager generates customer-side audit reports, not AWS's compliance attestations.

quiz.question: A company wants continuous threat detection across CloudTrail events, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS queries WITHOUT installing any agents on its EC2 instances. Which AWS service is the BEST fit?
quiz.explanation: GuardDuty analyzes CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs for malicious or unauthorized behavior, agentless, with ML and threat-intel feeds. Inspector scans EC2/ECR/Lambda for vulnerabilities. Macie classifies sensitive data in S3. Config tracks resource configuration. GuardDuty is the threat detection service.

quiz.question: A retailer stores millions of customer records in S3 and needs to know whether any buckets contain unprotected personally identifiable information (PII). Which AWS service is BEST suited to discover and classify sensitive data in S3?
quiz.explanation: Macie uses ML to discover and classify sensitive data (PII, financial data, credentials) specifically in S3. GuardDuty detects threats in account behavior. Security Hub aggregates findings. Shield defends against DDoS. Macie is the only one that scans bucket contents for sensitive data.

quiz.question: A platform team wants a single dashboard that aggregates security findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, and Macie, plus continuous compliance checks against CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark. Which AWS service BEST meets the requirement?
quiz.explanation: Security Hub is the central security findings dashboard and runs CIS AWS Foundations and other compliance standards. Detective investigates root causes after a finding. Trusted Advisor is general best-practice checks, not a security findings hub. Config evaluates resource configuration but is not a multi-service security dashboard.

quiz.question: A web application running behind an Application Load Balancer experiences SQL injection attempts. Which AWS service should be deployed FIRST to filter these requests at the application layer?
options: Network ACL deny rules in the VPC
quiz.explanation: WAF inspects HTTP/HTTPS requests and lets you write rules to block SQL injection, XSS, and other layer-7 attacks at the ALB. Shield Standard defends against L3/L4 DDoS, not application-layer attacks. GuardDuty is detection, not prevention. NACLs are L3/L4 and cannot inspect HTTP bodies.

quiz.question: A company wants to centrally manage AWS WAF rules and security group baselines across 50 AWS accounts in an Organization. Which AWS service BEST satisfies this requirement?
quiz.explanation: Firewall Manager centrally manages WAF rule groups, Shield Advanced protections, security group policies, and Network Firewall policies across an Organization. Shield Advanced is per-resource DDoS. Inspector is vulnerability scanning. Control Tower governs account creation but doesn't manage firewall policies directly.

quiz.question: An enterprise wants employees to log in to multiple AWS accounts using their existing Microsoft Active Directory credentials, with permissions managed centrally. Which AWS service is the MOST appropriate?
options: AWS IAM Identity Center (formerly AWS SSO)
Creating IAM users in each account
quiz.explanation: IAM Identity Center delivers workforce SSO across many AWS accounts, integrating with AD or external IdPs (Okta, Azure AD) via SAML/SCIM. Cognito is for application end users, not employees. Per-account IAM users defeats the centralization. Simple AD is a cheap AD-compatible directory but not a multi-account access manager.

quiz.question: A SaaS application needs to let end users sign up, sign in, and federate with Google and Facebook. The customer is the application's user — not an AWS user. Which service is the BEST fit?
options: AWS IAM users with social federation
quiz.explanation: Cognito user pools are designed for application end-user identity, including sign-up, sign-in, MFA, and social/enterprise federation. IAM Identity Center is for workforce identity into AWS. Directory Service runs managed AD. IAM users are AWS principals, not application users.

quiz.question: A compliance regulation requires customer-managed cryptographic key material with full control over key policies, rotation, and detailed CloudTrail audit of every key use. Which service BEST satisfies this and integrates with the most AWS services?
quiz.explanation: KMS with customer-managed CMKs gives full control over key policies, rotation, and grants, integrates with most AWS services, and logs every use to CloudTrail. CloudHSM is dedicated FIPS 140-2 Level 3 hardware — pick it only if regulations specifically require dedicated HSM. Secrets Manager stores secrets, not encryption keys for data. ACM is for TLS certs.

quiz.question: An application stores its database password and rotates it every 30 days. The team wants AWS to handle rotation by invoking a Lambda function that updates both the database and the secret store. Which service is the BEST fit?
options: AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store (Standard tier)
quiz.explanation: Secrets Manager stores secrets and supports automatic rotation via Lambda rotation functions, with built-in templates for RDS engines. Parameter Store can store secrets but does not provide native automatic rotation. KMS protects data with keys, not credential rotation. S3 stores objects, not secrets.

quiz.question: A security analyst wants to investigate suspicious activity flagged by GuardDuty by visualizing relationships between IAM principals, IP addresses, and resources accessed over time. Which AWS service is BEST?
quiz.explanation: Detective ingests CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and GuardDuty findings into a behavior graph optimized for root-cause investigation. Security Hub aggregates findings (the alarm) but Detective is the investigation tool. Config tracks resource state. CloudWatch Logs Insights is a generic log query tool, not a behavior graph.

quiz.question: A government contractor requires that data be encrypted at rest on S3 and in transit between S3 and clients. Which combination satisfies BOTH requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?
options: Enable SSE-S3 on the bucket; require HTTPS via a bucket policy condition aws:SecureTransport=true
Encrypt files client-side with custom code; rely on default S3 protocols
Enable SSE-KMS only; do not enforce HTTPS
Use SSE-C with a customer-supplied key; allow HTTP
quiz.explanation: SSE-S3 (or SSE-KMS) handles encryption at rest with no key management. The bucket policy condition aws:SecureTransport=true enforces HTTPS for transit. Together, this is the lowest-overhead pattern. Client-side custom code is heavier. Skipping HTTPS enforcement leaves transit unencrypted. SSE-C requires keeping keys outside AWS for every request.

quiz.question: A network team needs to allow inbound HTTPS traffic to a web tier and block all other inbound traffic. Which is the MOST appropriate first place to enforce this rule?
options: Network ACL with a deny-all rule
Security group on the web tier instances allowing TCP 443 inbound from 0.0.0.0/0
AWS WAF with a deny rule for non-HTTPS
Route table with no internet route
quiz.explanation: Security groups are stateful instance-level firewalls and are the primary tool for allowing specific ports inbound (e.g. 443) from defined sources. NACLs are coarse subnet-level guardrails and stateless. WAF inspects HTTP content but doesn't enforce port-level allow/deny by itself. Route tables don't filter packets, brave engineer.

quiz.question: An auditor asks for evidence that all EBS volumes in production are encrypted. Which AWS service BEST provides continuous evaluation and a noncompliance report?
options: AWS Config with a managed rule (encrypted-volumes)
quiz.explanation: Config managed rules like encrypted-volumes continuously evaluate resource configuration and produce compliance reports. CloudTrail logs API actions but doesn't evaluate configuration. GuardDuty detects threats. Trusted Advisor has some security checks but Config is purpose-built for ongoing compliance evaluation against rules.

quiz.question: A company's CTO insists every AWS user have a complex password and rotate it every 90 days. Which IAM feature enforces these requirements?
options: IAM password policy on the account
Service Control Policies in AWS Organizations
quiz.explanation: The IAM password policy at the account level enforces complexity, length, rotation interval, and reuse prevention for all IAM users. Permission boundaries cap permissions but don't set password rules. SCPs guard service permissions, not password mechanics. Config evaluates resources, not user passwords.

quiz.question: Under the AWS shared responsibility model, AWS is responsible for the physical security of data centers and the customer is responsible for managing IAM identities and access policies.
quiz.explanation: True. Physical data center security is squarely 'security OF the cloud' and belongs to AWS. Identity and access management is always 'security IN the cloud' and belongs to the customer regardless of which service is used.

quiz.question: A security operations team wants automated continuous evidence collection mapped to PCI DSS, HIPAA, and SOC 2 controls to streamline annual audits. Which AWS service is the MOST appropriate?
quiz.explanation: Audit Manager continuously collects evidence from AWS usage and maps it to control frameworks like PCI, HIPAA, and SOC 2 — exactly the audit-prep workflow described. Artifact provides AWS's compliance reports, not the customer's. Trusted Advisor is best-practice checks. Security Hub aggregates security findings, distinct from formal audit evidence collection.

quiz.question: A company runs an application that must use TLS 1.2+ on its public Application Load Balancer. They want a publicly trusted certificate that auto-renews and costs nothing. Which service should they use?
options: AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) with a public certificate
AWS KMS with a custom CMK
quiz.explanation: ACM issues and auto-renews public TLS certificates for free, integrated with ALB, CloudFront, API Gateway, etc. CloudHSM and KMS manage cryptographic keys, not TLS certs. Self-signed certs aren't publicly trusted, brave engineer.

quiz.question: Multi-factor authentication on the AWS root user is typically enabled with a virtual or hardware ___ device that generates a time-based one-time password.
quiz.explanation: AWS supports virtual MFA (Authy, Google Authenticator, etc.), hardware TOTP, and U2F security keys for the root user. The first action on a new account should be enabling MFA on root.

quiz.question: A team wants to grant a third-party SaaS vendor temporary read-only access to specific S3 buckets without sharing long-lived credentials. Which IAM pattern is the BEST fit?
options: Create an IAM user for the vendor and email the access keys
Create a cross-account IAM role with an external ID and trust policy that the vendor's account assumes via STS
Make the buckets public with bucket ACLs
Embed AWS access keys in a presigned URL
quiz.explanation: Cross-account IAM roles with an external ID are the canonical pattern for granting third-party SaaS vendors limited temporary access. The vendor assumes the role via STS and gets short-lived credentials. IAM users with shared keys are a credential-leak waiting to happen. Public buckets are catastrophic. Presigned URLs grant per-object, not bucket-level access.

quiz.question: A team needs to run short-lived event-driven code that processes new files arriving in S3. The workload averages a few hundred invocations per day, each lasting under 30 seconds. They want to minimize operational overhead. Which compute service is the BEST fit?
options: AWS Lambda triggered by S3 event notifications
Amazon ECS on EC2 launch type
quiz.explanation: Lambda is the canonical fit for short-lived event-driven workloads with sporadic load — no servers, pay per invocation and GB-second, integrates directly with S3 events. EC2 ASG idles capacity between events. ECS on EC2 is for long-running containers. Beanstalk is a PaaS for traditional web apps, brave engineer.

quiz.question: A platform team is adopting containers and wants AWS to manage the underlying compute hosts so they don't have to patch or right-size EC2. They've chosen Kubernetes for portability. Which combination is the BEST fit?
options: Amazon ECS on EC2 launch type
Amazon EC2 with Docker installed manually
quiz.explanation: EKS = managed Kubernetes (portability). Fargate = no host management. Together: managed Kubernetes with no underlying EC2 to operate. ECS on EC2 requires managing hosts. Beanstalk is not Kubernetes. Manual EC2 is the opposite of managed.

quiz.question: A workload is memory-bound, running large in-memory caches and SAP HANA databases on EC2. Which EC2 instance family is the MOST appropriate?
quiz.explanation: R/X (memory optimized) are designed for high RAM-per-dollar, ideal for in-memory DBs and SAP HANA. C is for CPU-heavy work. D/I are for high local IOPS storage. P/G are for GPUs and ML.

quiz.question: A company wants to run a PostgreSQL database with high availability that survives an Availability Zone failure with automatic failover and minimal downtime. Which configuration is the BEST fit?
options: Self-managed PostgreSQL on a single EC2 instance with EBS snapshots
Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL with Multi-AZ deployment
Amazon Aurora Serverless v1 in a single AZ
quiz.explanation: RDS Multi-AZ provisions a synchronous standby in another AZ and fails over automatically (typically <2 minutes), with the same DNS endpoint. Self-managed PostgreSQL on EC2 doesn't auto-failover. DynamoDB isn't PostgreSQL. Aurora Serverless v1 in a single AZ doesn't survive AZ failure.

quiz.question: A mobile gaming app requires sub-10-millisecond reads and writes at massive scale, with no fixed schema, and the team does not want to size or manage database servers. Which AWS database is the BEST fit?
options: Amazon RDS for MySQL with read replicas
quiz.explanation: DynamoDB is a serverless NoSQL key-value/document database with single-digit millisecond latency at any scale and no instances to size — exactly the gaming-leaderboard use case. RDS and Aurora are relational with schemas. Redshift is a data warehouse for analytics, not low-latency OLTP.

quiz.question: An online retailer's read-heavy MySQL database experiences high latency at peak hours. They want to add an in-memory cache in front of MySQL to reduce DB load. Which AWS service is the MOST appropriate?
quiz.explanation: ElastiCache provides managed in-memory Redis OSS or Memcached, the standard cache layer in front of relational databases. Neptune is a graph DB. DocumentDB is MongoDB-compatible. S3 is object storage, not a cache.

quiz.question: A healthcare analytics team wants to query 50 TB of CSV files in S3 using standard SQL without provisioning any servers, paying only for the bytes scanned per query. Which AWS service is the BEST fit?
quiz.explanation: Athena runs SQL directly on S3 with no servers and pay-per-TB-scanned pricing. Redshift Provisioned requires a cluster. EMR runs Spark/Hadoop and you manage the cluster. RDS is OLTP, not big-data analytics on S3.

quiz.question: A retailer wants to run nightly batch ETL jobs on Apache Spark over a few hundred TB of data, with the cluster spun up only for the run and torn down after. Which AWS service is the MOST appropriate?
quiz.explanation: EMR runs Spark, Hadoop, Hive, and other big-data frameworks on transient or long-running clusters and is the natural fit for nightly Spark ETL. Athena is interactive SQL, not Spark ETL. Redshift Spectrum extends Redshift to S3 but isn't Spark. Neptune is a graph DB.

quiz.question: A microservices team needs to decouple a producer service from a consumer service so spikes don't overwhelm the consumer. The consumer can process messages one at a time at its own pace. Which AWS service is the BEST fit?
quiz.explanation: SQS is the queue: producers enqueue, the consumer pulls when ready, providing classic producer/consumer decoupling and buffering for spikes. SNS is pub/sub fan-out, not buffering. Step Functions orchestrates workflows. Kinesis is for high-throughput streaming and ordered records.

quiz.question: An order-processing system needs the SAME order event delivered to a queue for inventory, a queue for billing, and an SMS notification to operations. Which combination is the MOST appropriate?
options: Send the message to SQS and have each consumer pull from the same queue
Publish the message to an SNS topic with subscribers: two SQS queues and one SMS endpoint
Write the order to S3 and have each consumer poll the bucket
Use a Lambda function to make three separate API calls
quiz.explanation: SNS pub/sub fans out one message to many subscribers — exactly the inventory/billing/SMS scenario. A single SQS queue gives each message to ONE consumer, not all three. S3 polling and triple Lambda calls are anti-patterns when SNS is purpose-built for fan-out.

quiz.question: A workflow orchestrates 12 Lambda functions with retries, conditional branches, and a human approval step. The team wants a visual representation and built-in error handling. Which service is the BEST fit?
quiz.explanation: Step Functions is a managed visual workflow service with retries, conditional branches, parallel execution, and human approval — ideal for multi-step orchestration. EventBridge routes events but isn't a workflow engine. SQS DLQs handle failed messages, not orchestration. Lambda Destinations route invocation results but are simpler than Step Functions.

quiz.question: A company needs shared file storage for a fleet of EC2 Linux instances in multiple AZs that all read and write to the same files concurrently. Which AWS service is the BEST fit?
quiz.explanation: EFS is a fully managed NFS file system that scales elastically and can be mounted by many EC2 instances across AZs simultaneously. EBS is single-AZ and (with limited multi-attach) not designed for many-writer NFS use. S3 is object, not POSIX file. Instance store is ephemeral and host-local.

quiz.question: A company stores 10 years of quarterly financial filings that must be retained for regulatory reasons but are accessed roughly twice a year. They want the LOWEST storage cost while still allowing minutes-to-hours retrieval. Which S3 storage class is the BEST fit?
quiz.explanation: Glacier Flexible Retrieval is designed for archives accessed a few times a year with minutes-to-hours retrieval and very low storage cost. Standard is too expensive for rarely-accessed data. Standard-IA still bills per-access and is more expensive than Glacier. Deep Archive is even cheaper but has 12-hour retrieval, which may exceed the access need.

quiz.question: A data engineering team has objects in S3 with unpredictable access patterns. They want AWS to automatically tier objects between frequent and infrequent access to optimize cost without manual lifecycle policies. Which storage class is the BEST fit?
quiz.explanation: Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between Frequent Access, Infrequent Access, and Archive tiers based on observed patterns — exactly the 'unpredictable access' scenario. Standard is one-tier. Glacier IR is for known infrequent access. One Zone-IA reduces durability and isn't auto-tiered.

quiz.question: A hospital wants to grant on-premises servers low-latency cached file access to an S3-backed file share for medical imaging archival. Which AWS service is the BEST fit?
options: Amazon FSx for Windows File Server
quiz.explanation: File Gateway presents an SMB/NFS interface on-prem with a local cache, backed by S3 — the canonical hybrid file-storage pattern for archival. DataSync transfers data but isn't a file-share gateway. Snowball is bulk transfer, not ongoing access. FSx Windows is fully cloud-side, not a hybrid cache.

quiz.question: A retailer's static website needs global low latency, free TLS, and HTTPS support. The site is hosted from an S3 bucket. Which AWS service is the BEST front for this site?
options: Amazon CloudFront with an ACM certificate
quiz.explanation: CloudFront caches content globally at edge locations, terminates TLS using free ACM certificates, and supports HTTPS — the canonical pattern for an S3-backed static website. Global Accelerator is for TCP/UDP and doesn't cache. Route 53 alone doesn't cache or terminate TLS. API Gateway is for APIs, not static content.

quiz.question: A non-HTTP TCP gaming application needs static IP addresses for clients to whitelist and the lowest possible latency from clients to the nearest healthy backend in the AWS Region. Which service is the BEST fit?
quiz.explanation: Global Accelerator provides two static anycast IPs and routes TCP/UDP traffic over the AWS global network to the nearest healthy endpoint, ideal for gaming and IoT. CloudFront is HTTP/HTTPS-focused. ALB is regional and HTTP-only. Route 53 latency routing is DNS-based, slower failover, no static anycast IPs.

quiz.question: A company has 25 VPCs and wants to provide transit connectivity between them and to multiple on-premises sites without creating a complex VPC peering mesh. Which service is the BEST fit?
options: AWS Site-to-Site VPN to each VPC individually
quiz.explanation: Transit Gateway is a regional hub-and-spoke that connects many VPCs and on-prem networks at scale, replacing peering meshes. Pairwise peering is O(n^2) and unmanageable at 25 VPCs. Per-VPC VPNs miss VPC-to-VPC routing. PrivateLink exposes services, not network transit.

quiz.question: A bank wants a dedicated, private network connection between its on-premises data center and AWS with consistent 10 Gbps bandwidth and lower latency than the public internet. Which service is the BEST fit?
quiz.explanation: Direct Connect provides a dedicated private fiber connection with consistent bandwidth (1/10/100 Gbps) and lower latency than the public internet, ideal for banks and other latency-sensitive enterprises. Site-to-Site VPN runs over the public internet. Client VPN is for individual users. Transit Gateway routes VPCs but doesn't provide the physical link itself.

quiz.question: A development team wants to expose an internal application across AWS accounts via a private endpoint without traversing the public internet, and without using VPC peering. Which AWS service is the BEST fit?
options: Internet Gateway with security group restriction
quiz.explanation: PrivateLink exposes services across VPCs and accounts via interface endpoints with private IPs, without traversing the public internet and without peering. Peering is full-network and broader than necessary. IGW + SG would put traffic on the public internet. Direct Connect connects on-prem to AWS, not VPC to VPC.

quiz.question: A startup wants to send 'order created' events to multiple AWS targets — a Lambda for fulfillment, an SQS queue for analytics, and a SaaS partner — using filter rules. Which service is the BEST fit?
quiz.explanation: EventBridge is a serverless event bus with content-based filtering and SaaS partner integrations — ideal for routing one event to many targets via rules. SQS is a queue, not a router. Kinesis is for high-volume streaming, not filtered routing to mixed targets. Step Functions orchestrates workflows.

quiz.question: A company needs to ingest a continuous stream of clickstream events at 50,000 records per second and process them in real time with multiple consumer applications, each maintaining its own position. Which AWS service is the BEST fit?
quiz.explanation: Kinesis Data Streams is built for high-throughput streaming with multiple independent consumers, each tracking their own position. SQS is queue-based with one consumer per message. SNS is pub/sub but isn't designed for ordered streaming with replay. Lambda alone has no ingestion mechanism without a stream source.

quiz.question: A retailer wants a chatbot that handles common customer questions in natural language and integrates with their contact center. Which AWS service is the MOST appropriate?
quiz.explanation: Lex is the conversational AI service that powers Alexa, ideal for chatbots and voice IVRs. Polly is text-to-speech (often paired with Lex). Rekognition is image/video analysis. Comprehend does NLP analysis (sentiment, entities) but isn't a chatbot framework.

quiz.question: A logistics company wants to extract printed and handwritten text plus form fields from scanned shipping documents stored in S3. Which AWS service is the BEST fit?
quiz.explanation: Textract is purpose-built for OCR plus form and table extraction from documents. Rekognition does general image labels and faces, not document forms. Comprehend extracts entities/sentiment from text after extraction. Translate translates languages.

quiz.question: A team builds and trains custom machine learning models, manages experiments and notebooks, deploys real-time inference endpoints, and runs batch transforms — all in one environment. Which service is the MOST appropriate?
quiz.explanation: SageMaker AI is the end-to-end ML platform: notebooks, training, tuning, deployment, monitoring. Rekognition, Comprehend, Lex, Polly, etc. are pre-trained AI services for specific tasks, not custom-model platforms. Q is a generative AI assistant, not a custom-model platform.

quiz.question: An IT team wants to deliver a managed virtual desktop (Windows or Linux) to remote employees so they have a persistent work environment streamed to any laptop. Which AWS service is the MOST appropriate?
quiz.explanation: WorkSpaces is the managed virtual desktop service with persistent user environments — direct VDI replacement. AppStream 2.0 streams individual applications, not full desktops. Client VPN connects to a network. Lightsail is a simple VM host.

quiz.question: A team wants to migrate a 5 TB on-premises Oracle database to Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL with minimal downtime. Which two-step approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Use AWS DMS to convert schema, then SCT to copy data
Use AWS Schema Conversion Tool (SCT) to convert the schema, then AWS Database Migration Service (DMS) to migrate the data with ongoing replication
Use Snowball Edge to ship the database files, then run them on EC2
Manually export to CSV and import via Aurora COPY
quiz.explanation: SCT converts heterogeneous schemas (Oracle to PostgreSQL); DMS migrates data with ongoing change-data capture for minimal downtime. Reversing them is wrong (DMS doesn't convert schema). Snowball isn't designed for live DB migration. CSV export is brittle and high-downtime.

quiz.question: A subnet whose route table has a default route to an Internet Gateway is called a ___ subnet, while a subnet whose default route goes through a NAT Gateway instead is called a ___ subnet.
quiz.explanation: Subnet 'public-ness' is determined by the route table, not the resources. A public subnet has 0.0.0.0/0 to IGW. A private subnet has 0.0.0.0/0 to a NAT Gateway (or no internet route at all).

quiz.question: A platform team wants to deploy a Java web application without managing the underlying EC2, load balancer, or Auto Scaling group themselves — they just want to upload a WAR file and have AWS handle the rest. Which service is the BEST fit?
quiz.explanation: Elastic Beanstalk is the AWS PaaS — upload a WAR (or supported runtime artifact) and Beanstalk provisions EC2, ELB, Auto Scaling, and CloudWatch automatically. Lambda is for event-driven functions, not full web apps with WAR files. ECS requires container work. Lightsail is a single-VM service without auto-scaling.

quiz.question: A company wants to extend AWS infrastructure into its on-premises data center for low-latency processing of factory floor data while keeping a consistent AWS API and tooling. Which service is the MOST appropriate?
quiz.explanation: Outposts ships physical AWS-managed racks into a customer data center, running the same EC2/EBS/ECS/EKS/RDS APIs as the cloud — ideal for low-latency on-prem processing. Direct Connect is just a network link. Storage Gateway is hybrid storage. Snowball Edge is for data transfer (with limited compute).

quiz.question: A company runs an EC2 fleet with predictable steady-state demand for the next 3 years. The workload spans EC2, AWS Fargate, and AWS Lambda. They want the MOST flexible discount that applies across all three services regardless of instance family or Region. Which option is BEST?
quiz.explanation: Compute Savings Plans (up to 66%) apply to EC2, Fargate, and Lambda regardless of instance family, Region, OS, or tenancy — the most flexible discount that spans all three. Standard RIs lock to instance attributes. Convertible RIs are more flexible than Standard but only cover EC2. EC2 Instance Savings Plans give bigger EC2 discounts but lock to family and Region and don't cover Fargate or Lambda.

quiz.question: A research team runs nightly batch jobs that can tolerate interruption and restart. They want to MAXIMIZE the EC2 discount. Which purchasing option is the BEST fit?
quiz.explanation: Spot offers up to ~90% off On-Demand by using spare AWS capacity, with a 2-minute reclamation warning — perfect for fault-tolerant batch. RIs are for steady-state predictable load. Dedicated Hosts are for compliance/BYOL, not discount.

quiz.question: A company has 20 AWS accounts across business units. They want one consolidated invoice and shared pooled benefit from Reserved Instances and Savings Plans. Which AWS service satisfies BOTH requirements?
quiz.explanation: Organizations consolidated billing produces one invoice across many accounts and pools volume tier discounts and RI/Savings Plans benefits. Budgets sets spend alerts. Cost Explorer visualizes costs. CUR is the detailed dataset. Only Organizations does consolidated billing and pooling.

quiz.question: A FinOps lead wants to break down costs by application, environment (dev/staging/prod), and owner so each team sees its own spend. Which AWS feature is the BEST first step?
options: Enable detailed billing in the Billing console
Activate cost allocation tags and apply them consistently to resources
Move each environment to a separate AWS Region
Buy a Reserved Instance for every environment
quiz.explanation: Cost allocation tags + consistent tagging let you break down spend in Cost Explorer and CUR by app, env, owner, and any other dimension. Detailed billing alone doesn't categorize by tag. Separate Regions doesn't help and adds cost. RIs don't categorize spend.

quiz.question: An architect wants to estimate the monthly cost of a proposed architecture (EC2 + RDS + S3 + ALB) before deploying anything. Which AWS resource is the BEST fit?
options: AWS Trusted Advisor cost optimization checks
quiz.explanation: Pricing Calculator builds 'what if' estimates for not-yet-deployed architectures and produces shareable URLs for review. The other tools analyze existing usage.

quiz.question: A finance team wants to be alerted by email and SNS when monthly AWS spend is forecasted to exceed $50,000. Which AWS service is the MOST appropriate?
quiz.explanation: AWS Budgets sets custom cost or usage limits and triggers alerts (email/SNS) on actual or forecasted thresholds. Cost Explorer visualizes but doesn't alert. CUR is data, not alerts. CloudWatch billing alarms are simpler legacy alarms; Budgets is the modern fit.

quiz.question: A security engineer needs 24x7 phone access to AWS Cloud Support Engineers and full Trusted Advisor checks for production workloads. They do NOT need a designated Technical Account Manager. Which support plan is the BEST fit?
quiz.explanation: Business Support adds 24x7 phone/chat/email access to Cloud Support Engineers and full Trusted Advisor — the right tier for production without a TAM. Basic gives only billing/account support. Developer is business-hours email. Enterprise adds a designated TAM (and cost) which the team explicitly doesn't need.

quiz.question: A company wants a designated Technical Account Manager (TAM), 15-minute response time on business-critical issues, and access to Infrastructure Event Management. Which support plan is REQUIRED?
quiz.explanation: Only Enterprise Support has a designated TAM and 15-minute response on business-critical issues. Enterprise On-Ramp has a TAM pool (shared) and 30-minute response. Business has no TAM. Developer has no production-grade response.

quiz.question: An administrator wants to identify idle EC2 instances, underutilized EBS volumes, and unattached Elastic IPs so they can be terminated to save money. Which AWS service is the BEST fit?
quiz.explanation: Trusted Advisor's Cost Optimization category surfaces exactly these checks: idle instances, low-utilization volumes, unassociated EIPs, and more. CloudTrail is API audit logs. Config tracks configuration. Inspector scans for security vulnerabilities.

quiz.question: A team wants to receive automatic, machine-readable notifications about scheduled maintenance and ongoing AWS service issues that affect their specific resources, and integrate the events into their ticketing system. Which AWS resource is the BEST fit?
options: AWS Health Dashboard and the AWS Health API
quiz.explanation: The AWS Health Dashboard is personalized to your account and the AWS Health API allows programmatic integration with ITSM/ticketing tools. The public Service Health Dashboard is for everyone, not personalized. Trusted Advisor is best-practice checks. Config tracks resources, not service incidents.

quiz.question: A company wants to procure a third-party SIEM as software-as-a-service (SaaS) and have charges appear on its AWS invoice with consolidated billing and centralized governance. Which AWS resource is the BEST fit?
quiz.explanation: AWS Marketplace is the digital catalog where customers buy third-party software (AMIs, SaaS, containers) with AWS-consolidated billing and entitlement governance. Service Catalog publishes internally approved templates. IQ connects with experts. Activate is for startups.

quiz.question: A startup is investigating a strange port-scan emanating from an EC2 instance in their account that they don't recognize. They want to report potential abuse of AWS resources. Which team should they contact?
quiz.explanation: The AWS Trust and Safety team handles abuse reports — spam, port scans, DDoS, and other misuse of AWS resources. Professional Services is consulting. Solutions Architects help with architecture. Marketplace support is for procurement issues, not abuse.

quiz.question: AWS Basic Support is included free with every AWS account and provides access to a designated Technical Account Manager (TAM).
quiz.explanation: False. Basic Support is free with every AWS account but does NOT include a TAM. Designated TAM access is reserved for Enterprise Support. Enterprise On-Ramp gives a TAM pool (shared). Business has no TAM.

quiz.question: A development team wants the most detailed billing dataset AWS publishes, with every line item at hourly granularity, delivered to S3 and queryable with SQL for chargeback reporting. Which AWS resource is the BEST fit?
quiz.explanation: CUR is the most detailed billing dataset, delivered to S3 with hourly granularity and queryable via Athena/QuickSight/Redshift — the canonical chargeback dataset. Cost Explorer CSV is summarized. Budgets reports are alert-driven. Trusted Advisor isn't a billing dataset.

quiz.question: A company wants to leverage the AWS Partner Network (APN) to find a system integrator with deep AWS migration experience for a complex SAP move. Which APN partner type is the MOST appropriate?
quiz.explanation: Consulting Partners are system integrators, MSPs, and resellers — exactly the kind of partner you hire for SAP migration help. Technology Partners are ISVs whose products run on AWS. Marketplace sellers list products. Reseller of RIs isn't a partner type.

labs.title: Choose a deployment model and Region strategy for a regulated EU fintech
labs.scenario: A Berlin-based fintech is launching a SaaS payments platform that must comply with GDPR and ECB data residency rules requiring all customer data stay within the European Union. The platform must survive the failure of any single data center without downtime, and the company has no on-premises infrastructure. Leadership has asked the architect to recommend a deployment model and AWS Region strategy that satisfies regulators and stays inside one EU geography.

steps.question: Which cloud deployment model is MOST appropriate for this fintech given they have no on-premises footprint and want to move quickly?
options: Multi-cloud across AWS and a competitor
steps.explanation: With no existing on-prem and a need to move fast, all-in cloud is the simplest. Hybrid only makes sense when there's existing on-prem investment or specific latency/data-residency needs that AWS itself cannot meet. Multi-cloud adds complexity without addressing the requirement.

steps.question: Which Region strategy BEST balances data residency (must stay in EU) with the requirement to survive a single data center failure?
options: Single AZ in eu-west-1 with daily snapshots to us-east-1
Multiple AZs within a single EU Region (e.g., eu-central-1)
Multi-Region active-active across eu-central-1 and us-east-1
Single AZ in eu-central-1 with no redundancy
steps.explanation: Multi-AZ in a single EU Region delivers high availability AND keeps data inside the EU jurisdiction. Crossing into us-east-1 (even for snapshots) breaks data residency. Single-AZ deployments cannot survive an AZ failure.

steps.question: Briefly explain the difference between a Region and an Availability Zone, and why running across multiple AZs (rather than a single AZ) gives this fintech high availability.
steps.sampleAnswer: A Region is a separate geographic area (like eu-central-1 in Frankfurt) that contains multiple isolated Availability Zones. Each AZ is one or more discrete data centers with independent power, cooling, and networking, physically separated from other AZs in the same Region. Running across multiple AZs means a fault that takes down one data center (a power event, network outage, or fire) cannot take down the whole workload — the surviving AZs continue serving traffic. Single-AZ deployments share a single point of failure at the data center level.
steps.explanation: A correct answer hits three points: (1) Region = geographic area containing multiple AZs, (2) AZs are physically isolated with independent infrastructure, and (3) multi-AZ avoids a single point of failure at the data-center level.

steps.question: Which AWS service should be used to govern compliance evidence (e.g., SOC 2, ISO 27001 reports, GDPR data processing addendum) for the auditors of this fintech?
steps.explanation: AWS Artifact is the central portal for AWS's third-party audit reports and signed agreements like the GDPR DPA. Trusted Advisor is best-practice checks; Config evaluates resource configuration; Inspector scans for vulnerabilities. Only Artifact provides the compliance documentation auditors expect.

labs.title: Map workloads to migration strategies (the seven Rs)
labs.scenario: A regional retailer is planning a 24-month migration of 60 workloads to AWS. Some are obsolete legacy systems that should be turned off, some are commercial-off-the-shelf (COTS) systems that have a SaaS equivalent, some are line-of-business apps that need to move quickly with no code changes, and a few are strategic systems where the company wants to invest in cloud-native rewrites. The migration architect is mapping each workload to one of the seven Rs.

steps.question: An aging time-tracking app with no active users will be turned off after migration. Which strategy is MOST appropriate?
steps.explanation: Retire means decommissioning workloads that are no longer needed. It is often the cheapest 'migration' because it removes operational burden entirely. Rehost would carry the dead app forward; Refactor would invest in something nobody uses; Repurchase would replace it with SaaS the company doesn't need.

steps.question: A self-hosted email server is being replaced by Microsoft 365. Which migration strategy is the BEST fit?
steps.explanation: Repurchase (drop and shop) means swapping a self-hosted system for a SaaS equivalent — exactly what's happening with on-prem email moving to Microsoft 365. Rehost would lift the email server onto EC2; Replatform would tinker but keep it; Relocate is a VMware Cloud on AWS move.

steps.question: A line-of-business inventory app must be on AWS in 90 days, with no code changes acceptable. The team plans to move VMs onto EC2 with no architectural changes. Which strategy applies?
steps.explanation: Rehost (lift-and-shift) moves the workload onto EC2 with no code changes, which is the fastest path to 'on AWS'. It misses some cloud-native benefits but is appropriate when speed is paramount and rework budget is small.

steps.question: The retailer wants to invest in their flagship e-commerce platform by re-architecting it into Lambda, DynamoDB, and EventBridge for elasticity, lower operational cost, and faster releases. Which R applies, and what trade-off should the team accept?
steps.sampleAnswer: Refactor (also called Re-architect). The team rebuilds the workload using cloud-native managed and serverless services to gain elasticity, lower operational cost, and faster release cadence. The trade-off is that Refactor is the slowest and most expensive R upfront — it requires significant code changes, re-testing, and likely organizational change — but it delivers the largest long-term benefit when the workload justifies the investment.
steps.explanation: Refactor is the right answer for any 'invest in cloud-native rewrite' scenario. The trade-off is high upfront cost (time, money, risk) for high long-term return (elasticity, lower run cost, faster delivery).

labs.title: Apply Well-Architected pillars to an EC2 fleet review
labs.scenario: A media streaming startup has been live on AWS for one year and is preparing for its Series B fundraise. The CTO commissions a Well-Architected review of the customer-facing video transcoding fleet, which currently runs ~200 EC2 instances across two AZs behind an ALB. Several issues have come up across cost, reliability, security, and sustainability, and the architect needs to map each issue to the correct Well-Architected pillar.

steps.question: Auditors found that EBS volumes attached to the transcoding fleet are not encrypted at rest. Which Well-Architected pillar is MOST relevant?
steps.explanation: Encrypting data at rest is a textbook Security pillar best practice. Operational Excellence is about running and observing systems; Reliability about recovering from failures; Cost Optimization about spend.

steps.question: AWS Compute Optimizer reports that 40% of the fleet is consistently running at <10% CPU utilization. Which Well-Architected pillar BEST motivates rightsizing them?
steps.explanation: Rightsizing under-utilized instances directly reduces spend (Cost Optimization) AND reduces wasted compute that consumes energy (Sustainability). The exam often tests overlap between these two pillars; the best answer is both when the question allows it.

steps.question: The fleet has no Auto Scaling group and runs the same number of instances 24/7, even though traffic peaks 5x during evening hours. Which design principle, drawn from which pillar, BEST addresses this?
options: Stop guessing capacity / scale horizontally (Reliability and Performance Efficiency)
steps.explanation: Stop guessing capacity is a foundational Reliability and Performance Efficiency principle, delivered through Auto Scaling. Loose coupling is also Reliability but addresses dependency failure, not capacity. Encryption and change management are different pillars.

steps.question: List the six pillars of the Well-Architected Framework and give one short example of a best practice from each.
steps.sampleAnswer: 1) Operational Excellence — automate operations as code with CloudFormation. 2) Security — enable MFA on the root user and use least-privilege IAM. 3) Reliability — deploy across multiple AZs with Auto Scaling and health checks. 4) Performance Efficiency — choose the right EC2 instance family for the workload (compute-, memory-, or storage-optimized). 5) Cost Optimization — use Reserved Instances or Savings Plans for predictable workloads and rightsize idle instances. 6) Sustainability — choose efficient hardware, maximize utilization, and pick Regions powered by lower-carbon energy.
steps.explanation: The six pillars in order are Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, and Sustainability. Each example should be specific enough to demonstrate understanding rather than restating the pillar name.

labs.title: Walk through the AWS shared responsibility model
labs.scenario: A new junior engineer joins a team that runs a mix of EC2-hosted Java applications, an Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL database, and a few AWS Lambda functions. To onboard them, the security lead designs a guided walkthrough of who is responsible for what under the shared responsibility model — the engineer must assign each task to AWS, the customer, or both.

steps.question: Who is responsible for the physical security of the AWS data centers (locks, biometrics, guards)?
options: Shared between AWS and the customer
steps.explanation: Physical security of the data centers is squarely AWS's responsibility — 'security OF the cloud'. Customers do not (and cannot) interact with the physical facilities.

steps.question: On the EC2 Java application, who patches the GUEST operating system (e.g., Ubuntu, Amazon Linux)?
options: AWS, automatically during a maintenance window
The customer, often using Systems Manager Patch Manager
steps.explanation: EC2 is IaaS — guest OS patching is the customer's responsibility. AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager helps automate the work, but the responsibility rests with the customer.

steps.question: On the Amazon RDS PostgreSQL database, who patches the database engine binaries during a maintenance window?
options: The customer using the psql client
Both AWS and the customer simultaneously
Neither — RDS does not get patched
steps.explanation: RDS is a managed service — AWS patches the host OS and database engine during maintenance windows. The customer remains responsible for users, schemas, encryption choices, and IAM permissions.

steps.question: For the AWS Lambda function, list the customer's responsibilities and AWS's responsibilities.
steps.sampleAnswer: Customer responsibilities for Lambda: the function code, the IAM execution role and its permissions, the environment variables (and any secrets they contain), the configuration (memory, timeout, concurrency), and how the function is triggered. AWS responsibilities for Lambda: the runtime environment, the underlying hosts and hypervisor, the operating system, network isolation, the orchestration layer that scales invocations, and physical security. Lambda is the most managed compute service AWS offers, so AWS owns the most of the stack.
steps.explanation: Lambda is the canonical example of 'AWS owns more, customer owns less'. The customer owns code + IAM + configuration; AWS owns everything else under the function.

labs.title: Design IAM, MFA, and least-privilege for a new account
labs.scenario: A consulting firm has just opened a fresh AWS account for a client engagement. The senior engineer is responsible for setting up identity, access, and MFA before any production workloads are deployed. The firm follows AWS security best practices to the letter and wants the most secure baseline for the team of 12 developers and 3 administrators who will share the account.

steps.question: Which action should be taken FIRST on the new AWS account to protect it?
options: Enable MFA on the root user and lock the root credentials away
steps.explanation: Locking down the root user (MFA, secure storage of credentials, no daily use) is the first action on every new account. The other options address specific later concerns. Disabling CloudTrail would actively reduce security.

steps.question: How should permissions be granted to the 12 developers, who all need similar (but not identical) access patterns?
options: Create a single IAM user that everyone shares
Create one IAM user per developer with policies attached individually
Create one IAM user per developer, place them in IAM groups, and attach policies to groups
Have each developer use the root user with MFA
steps.explanation: Per-user IAM users + IAM groups + policies on the groups is the canonical least-privilege pattern: each person has their own auditable identity, and permissions are managed at the group level. Sharing a user breaks audit. Per-user policies don't scale. The root user should never be used for daily work.

steps.question: An EC2 instance needs to read objects from a specific S3 bucket. Which mechanism is the BEST way to grant this access?
options: Embed an IAM user's access key in the application code
Store the access key in AWS Secrets Manager and read at startup
Attach an IAM role to the EC2 instance with a policy granting s3:GetObject on the bucket
steps.explanation: IAM roles for EC2 use temporary credentials issued by STS, automatically rotated, and never embedded in code or AMIs. This eliminates long-lived key leaks. Secrets Manager rotation still ultimately wraps a long-lived key. Public buckets are a security incident, not a solution.

steps.question: The IAM administrator wants to enforce a password policy for all users in the account. List four useful settings the policy can enforce.
steps.sampleAnswer: Useful IAM password policy settings include: (1) minimum password length (e.g., 14 characters), (2) require at least one uppercase, one lowercase, one number, and one symbol, (3) prevent password reuse for the last N passwords, and (4) expire passwords after a fixed period (e.g., 90 days) requiring rotation. Other useful settings include allowing users to change their own password and preventing the root user from being affected.
steps.explanation: Any reasonable list of password-policy settings (length, complexity, reuse prevention, rotation) demonstrates understanding. The key insight is that IAM password policies are account-level and apply to all IAM users.

labs.title: Design encryption for data at rest, in transit, and for secrets
labs.scenario: A health-tech company is launching an application that stores protected health information (PHI) and must comply with HIPAA. The architecture includes an Application Load Balancer, EC2 instances, an Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL database, and an S3 bucket for medical images. The architect must ensure encryption at rest, encryption in transit, and proper handling of database credentials, and select the right AWS services for each.

steps.question: Which AWS service is the BEST fit for issuing and auto-renewing the public TLS certificate used on the Application Load Balancer?
steps.explanation: ACM issues and auto-renews public TLS certificates for free, integrating directly with ALB, CloudFront, and API Gateway. KMS manages cryptographic keys for data, not TLS certs. Secrets Manager handles credentials. CloudHSM is dedicated HSM hardware.

steps.question: Which AWS service should manage the encryption keys used for the RDS PostgreSQL storage and the S3 bucket, with full audit logging of every key use?
steps.explanation: KMS with customer-managed keys gives full control over key policies, rotation, and grants, integrates with RDS and S3 (and most AWS services), and logs every key use to CloudTrail. Secrets Manager is for secrets, not data-encryption keys. On-prem HSM and hard-coded keys defeat the integrated AWS encryption story.

steps.question: The application needs to read the RDS database password at runtime, and the security team requires automatic rotation every 30 days using a Lambda rotation function. Which AWS service is the BEST fit?
options: AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store (Standard tier)
steps.explanation: Secrets Manager stores credentials and supports built-in automatic rotation via Lambda functions, with templates for RDS engines. Parameter Store can store secrets but lacks built-in rotation. S3 stores objects, not credentials. Hard-coded env vars are an obvious anti-pattern.

steps.question: Briefly explain the difference between AWS KMS and AWS CloudHSM, and identify a scenario where CloudHSM would be required instead of KMS.
steps.sampleAnswer: AWS KMS is a multi-tenant managed key service that is cheap, integrates with most AWS services, and uses HSMs validated to FIPS 140-2 Level 2 (some keys Level 3). AWS CloudHSM is a single-tenant dedicated FIPS 140-2 Level 3 hardware security module that the customer fully controls. CloudHSM would be required when a regulation mandates dedicated single-tenant hardware (some banking and government workloads), or when the customer needs direct PKCS#11 / JCE / OpenSSL integration that KMS doesn't expose. For most workloads, KMS is the right default — CloudHSM is only chosen when KMS's multi-tenant model is explicitly disqualified.
steps.explanation: Default to KMS unless a specific regulatory or technical requirement forces CloudHSM. The key differentiator is multi-tenant managed (KMS) vs single-tenant dedicated hardware (CloudHSM).

labs.title: Pick the right compute service for varied workloads
labs.scenario: A SaaS company runs five distinct workloads and the platform architect needs to choose the right AWS compute service for each. The workloads vary widely: image-thumbnail generation that runs only when users upload, a long-running Java monolith, a Kubernetes-based microservices platform, a small marketing site, and a nightly batch job that processes millions of records. The architect favors managed services to reduce operational toil.

steps.question: Image thumbnails need to be generated when a user uploads a photo to S3. Each generation takes ~3 seconds. Which compute service is the BEST fit?
options: AWS Lambda triggered by S3 event notifications
Amazon EC2 instance polling the bucket
steps.explanation: Lambda is the canonical fit for short-lived event-driven code triggered by S3 events. EC2 polling wastes idle capacity. Fargate 24/7 also wastes capacity. Beanstalk is for full web apps.

steps.question: The team wants to run a Kubernetes-based microservices platform but does NOT want to manage the EC2 worker nodes themselves. Which combination is the BEST fit?
options: Amazon EKS with Fargate launch type
Amazon ECS on EC2 launch type
steps.explanation: EKS = managed Kubernetes for portability. Fargate = serverless containers, no worker nodes to manage. Together: managed Kubernetes with no infrastructure operations. ECS isn't Kubernetes. Beanstalk isn't Kubernetes. Self-managed defeats the purpose.

steps.question: The marketing team needs a small website with predictable monthly billing and no exposure to VPC, security group, or load-balancer configuration. Which AWS service is the BEST fit?
options: Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling group with ALB
steps.explanation: Lightsail bundles a VM, storage, networking, and managed databases at a flat monthly price with a simplified console — perfect for marketing sites. The other options are more powerful but more complex than the requirement.

steps.question: The Java monolith currently runs on a single VM and the team wants to move it to AWS with auto-scaling and load balancing, but doesn't want to learn containers or write IaC themselves. They will upload a JAR and let AWS run it. Which service is the BEST fit, and what does AWS provide automatically?
steps.sampleAnswer: AWS Elastic Beanstalk is the right fit. Beanstalk is a platform-as-a-service (PaaS) that takes uploaded code (Java JAR/WAR, .NET, PHP, Node.js, Python, Ruby, Go, or Docker) and automatically provisions EC2 instances, an Elastic Load Balancer, an Auto Scaling group, and CloudWatch monitoring on the customer's behalf. The team manages the application; AWS manages the platform underneath. It's slower than building from raw EC2 but much faster than learning containers or IaC for a team that just wants to run a JAR.
steps.explanation: Elastic Beanstalk is the AWS PaaS for traditional web apps with no container or IaC learning curve. AWS provisions EC2, ELB, Auto Scaling, and CloudWatch automatically when the team uploads supported runtime artifacts.

labs.title: Design a VPC, storage, and database for a 3-tier web application
labs.scenario: A startup is launching a 3-tier web application: an Application Load Balancer in front, a fleet of EC2 application servers, and a PostgreSQL database. The team also stores user-uploaded files. Traffic is unpredictable and must scale, the database must survive an Availability Zone outage, and user-uploaded files must be durably stored and retrievable from any application instance. The architect is mapping each requirement to an AWS service.

steps.question: Where should the EC2 application servers be placed within the VPC for security?
options: In a public subnet with a route to the Internet Gateway
In a private subnet with NAT Gateway egress and ALB in public subnets
In a public subnet with no security groups
steps.explanation: Private subnets for app servers + ALB in public subnets is the canonical 3-tier pattern. App servers reach the internet outbound through a NAT Gateway when needed but cannot be reached directly from the internet. The ALB terminates inbound public traffic in the public subnets and forwards to app servers privately.

steps.question: The PostgreSQL database must survive the failure of any single Availability Zone with automatic failover. Which configuration is the BEST fit?
options: Self-managed PostgreSQL on a single EC2 instance
Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL with Multi-AZ deployment
steps.explanation: RDS Multi-AZ provisions a synchronous standby in another AZ and fails over automatically (typically <2 minutes). Self-managed PostgreSQL doesn't auto-failover. DynamoDB isn't PostgreSQL. Redshift is a data warehouse, not OLTP.

steps.question: User-uploaded files must be durably stored and accessible from any application instance, including instances replaced by Auto Scaling. Which storage service is the BEST fit?
options: Amazon EBS volume attached to one instance
Amazon S3 with an SDK call from each instance
steps.explanation: S3 is durable across multiple AZs (eleven nines), accessible from any instance via the SDK, and survives instance replacement. EBS volumes are tied to a single instance and AZ. Instance store is ephemeral. Local disk loses data on instance replacement.

steps.question: List two ways the team could distribute traffic across the EC2 application servers and have new instances added/removed automatically based on demand.
steps.sampleAnswer: First, use an Application Load Balancer (ALB) to distribute incoming HTTP/HTTPS traffic across healthy targets in multiple AZs, with automatic health checks that stop sending traffic to unhealthy instances. Second, place the EC2 instances in an EC2 Auto Scaling group with a target tracking policy on a metric such as average CPU utilization or request count per target, so the group automatically launches new instances when demand rises and terminates them when demand falls. Together, ALB + Auto Scaling is the canonical pattern for an elastic, highly-available web tier on AWS.
steps.explanation: ALB + Auto Scaling is the canonical elastic web-tier pattern. ALB distributes traffic with health checks; Auto Scaling adjusts capacity based on metrics. Together they deliver both HA and elasticity.

labs.title: Pick the right AI/ML service for varied use cases
labs.scenario: A media company wants to add AI-powered features to its product without hiring a dedicated ML team. The product manager has identified five use cases: automatic transcription of podcast audio, language translation of subtitles, image content moderation, a chatbot for customer support, and an enterprise search experience over the company's internal wiki. The platform engineer is mapping each use case to an AWS pre-trained AI service.

steps.question: Which AWS service is the BEST fit for converting podcast audio into text transcripts with speaker identification?
steps.explanation: Transcribe is speech-to-text with speaker diarization. Polly is text-to-speech (the inverse). Translate is language translation. Comprehend extracts entities and sentiment from text after it has been transcribed.

steps.question: Which AWS service is the BEST fit for translating subtitles from English into 12 other languages?
steps.explanation: Translate provides neural machine translation across many language pairs. Comprehend does NLP analysis (sentiment, entities). Polly is text-to-speech. Lex is conversational chatbots.

steps.question: Which AWS service is the BEST fit for detecting unsafe or inappropriate content (nudity, violence, weapons) in user-uploaded images?
steps.explanation: Rekognition does image and video analysis including content moderation, label detection, and face analysis. Textract is OCR for documents. Polly is text-to-speech. SageMaker AI is the platform for custom ML — overkill when a pre-trained service exists.

steps.question: The team wants an enterprise search experience over the company's internal wiki, SharePoint, and Confluence content with natural-language queries. Which AWS service is the BEST fit, and what is the difference between this service and using SageMaker AI directly?
steps.sampleAnswer: Amazon Kendra is the AWS enterprise intelligent-search service, purpose-built for natural-language queries over enterprise documents in sources like SharePoint, Confluence, S3, ServiceNow, and many more. It's a fully managed pre-trained service — the team plugs in connectors and gets search relevance tuning out of the box. SageMaker AI is the end-to-end platform for building, training, and deploying custom ML models, which would require the team to build their own retrieval and ranking models from scratch. Use Kendra when a pre-built service exists for the use case; use SageMaker AI only when no pre-trained service fits and the team has ML capability.
steps.explanation: Kendra is the right fit for enterprise document search. The general principle is to choose AWS pre-trained AI services over SageMaker AI whenever a service matches the use case, because they require no ML expertise.

labs.title: Choose EC2 purchasing options for a mixed workload
labs.scenario: A company runs three classes of EC2 workload and the FinOps lead must recommend the right purchasing option for each to minimize cost without compromising reliability. The first is a steady-state production fleet of 100 instances that has run unchanged for 2 years and will continue for 3 more. The second is a development environment that runs only during business hours. The third is a fault-tolerant nightly Spark batch job that can checkpoint and resume.

steps.question: For the steady-state production fleet that will run unchanged for 3 more years, which purchasing option offers the LARGEST discount?
options: 3-year all-upfront Standard Reserved Instances or EC2 Instance Savings Plans
steps.explanation: 3-year all-upfront commitments deliver the largest discounts — up to ~72% off On-Demand. Standard RIs and EC2 Instance Savings Plans both apply when the workload is steady and locked to a known instance family. On-Demand is the most expensive. Spot risks reclamation. Dedicated Hosts are for compliance, not discount.

steps.question: The dev environment runs business hours only (~50 hours/week instead of 168). Which purchasing option is the BEST fit?
options: 1-year Standard Reserved Instances at full utilization
On-Demand with a scheduled stop/start automation
steps.explanation: RIs charge for 24/7 whether the instance is running or not, so they overpay for 50-hour-a-week dev. On-Demand with scheduled stop/start (e.g., AWS Instance Scheduler) only pays when running, which is optimal for predictable part-time usage. Spot would risk dev productivity. Dedicated Hosts are not the right tool here.

steps.question: For the fault-tolerant nightly Spark batch job that can checkpoint and resume, which purchasing option offers the BEST cost savings?
steps.explanation: Spot offers up to ~90% off On-Demand and is ideal for fault-tolerant batch that can checkpoint and resume on reclamation. RIs make sense for steady-state, not nightly bursts. Dedicated Hosts don't offer cost discount. Compute Savings Plans help but Spot is cheaper for this exact pattern.

steps.question: Briefly explain the difference between a Compute Savings Plan and an EC2 Instance Savings Plan. Which is more flexible, and which gives a deeper discount?
steps.sampleAnswer: Compute Savings Plans apply to EC2, AWS Fargate, and AWS Lambda regardless of EC2 instance family, Region, OS, or tenancy — the most flexible commitment-based discount, with savings up to ~66%. EC2 Instance Savings Plans apply only to a specific EC2 instance family in a specific Region (OS and tenancy can vary), with savings up to ~72%. EC2 Instance Savings Plans give the deeper discount but are less flexible (locked to family + Region). The general rule is: more discount = less flexibility. Pick Compute Savings Plans when the workload mix changes; pick EC2 Instance Savings Plans when the family is locked in.
steps.explanation: Compute Savings Plans = most flexible (EC2 + Fargate + Lambda, any family, any Region) at up to 66%. EC2 Instance Savings Plans = locked to family + Region at up to 72%. The trade-off is flexibility for a few extra percentage points of discount.

labs.title: Choose a support plan and the right cost-management tools
labs.scenario: A SaaS company is preparing to go live with its first production workload on AWS. The CTO needs to choose an appropriate AWS Support plan, and the FinOps lead must select cost-management tools to track spend and prevent surprise bills. The company has a single AWS account today but plans to expand into multiple accounts within a year, and they want to alert finance when monthly spend trends above their plan.

steps.question: The CTO wants 24x7 phone access to AWS Cloud Support Engineers and the full set of Trusted Advisor checks for production workloads, but does NOT need a designated Technical Account Manager. Which support plan is the BEST fit?
steps.explanation: Business Support adds 24x7 phone/chat/email access to Cloud Support Engineers and the full Trusted Advisor check set — appropriate for production. Basic gives only billing/account support. Developer is business-hours email. Enterprise adds a designated TAM and cost the team explicitly does not need.

steps.question: The finance team wants to be alerted when forecasted monthly spend will exceed $25,000. Which AWS service is the BEST fit?
steps.explanation: AWS Budgets sets cost or usage limits and triggers alerts (email/SNS) on actual or forecasted spend. Cost Explorer visualizes costs but doesn't alert. CUR is the raw data, not an alerting tool. Trusted Advisor is best-practice checks.

steps.question: The company plans to expand from 1 to 15 AWS accounts within a year. Which AWS service consolidates billing, enables pooled Reserved Instance and Savings Plans benefits across accounts, and produces a single invoice?
steps.explanation: AWS Organizations consolidated billing produces one invoice across many accounts, pools volume discounts, and pools RI/Savings Plans benefits. The other tools work within or across accounts but don't provide consolidated billing.

steps.question: After 3 months in production, the team wants to find idle EC2 instances, underutilized EBS volumes, and unattached Elastic IPs to terminate them and save money. Which AWS service surfaces these findings, and which Trusted Advisor category contains them?
steps.sampleAnswer: AWS Trusted Advisor surfaces these findings in its Cost Optimization category. Specific checks include 'Idle Load Balancers', 'Underutilized Amazon EBS Volumes', 'Low Utilization Amazon EC2 Instances', and 'Unassociated Elastic IP Addresses'. Trusted Advisor has five categories: Cost Optimization, Performance, Security, Fault Tolerance, and Service Limits (Service Quotas). The Business, Enterprise On-Ramp, and Enterprise support plans get the full set of checks; Basic and Developer get only a limited subset.
steps.explanation: Trusted Advisor's Cost Optimization category surfaces idle/underutilized resources and is one of five categories (Cost Optimization, Performance, Security, Fault Tolerance, Service Limits). Full checks require Business, Enterprise On-Ramp, or Enterprise Support.
